Example 1:
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Goddard Space Flight Center, is seeking a contractor to provide engineering, integration, and test support services for an upcoming Earth science mission. The nature of the work, which includes systems engineering, instrument calibration, software verification, environmental testing, launch support, and post-launch on-orbit operations, necessitates a large, multidisciplinary team. The contractor shall maintain a team of at minimum fourteen (14) engineers and scientists throughout the performance period, with additional personnel required during critical mission phases such as environmental test campaigns and launch operations. The core team shall include systems engineers, mechanical engineers, electrical engineers, software engineers, and integration and test specialists with demonstrated spaceflight heritage. The offeror shall provide a staffing matrix in the proposal that maps each of the fourteen required positions to an individual, identifies their role, qualifications, and availability by contract phase. The Government understands that mission phases will drive variable workload and reserves the right to request surge staffing above the fourteen-person baseline; however, the minimum staffing level shall not fall below fourteen individuals at any time during the base period of performance.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, is issuing this solicitation for nuclear security program support services at a classified facility in the Southeast United States. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, announces this solicitation for facility operations and maintenance services for a portfolio of seventeen federally owned buildings located in the National Capital Region. The combined gross square footage of the portfolio exceeds 4.2 million square feet, encompassing office buildings, courthouses, and a federal laboratory campus. To ensure adequate coverage and rapid response to maintenance needs across this geographically dispersed portfolio, the contractor shall provide a minimum staffing complement of thirty (30) full-time personnel. Required positions include a Contract Manager, an Operations Manager, six (6) licensed journeyman electricians, four (4) HVAC mechanics holding EPA 608 Universal certification, three (3) plumbers, two (2) elevator mechanics, two (2) fire suppression system technicians, a locksmith, a roofing specialist, and sufficient general maintenance workers to support all preventive and corrective maintenance tasks. The contractor shall also provide administrative staff to support work order management and reporting. All personnel shall be physically located within the National Capital Region and available to respond to emergency calls within two (2) hours of notification. Staffing plans must demonstrate no gaps in coverage for any building within the portfolio.

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, Office of Marine and Aviation Operations, issues this Request for Proposal for scientific data collection, oceanographic survey support, and fleet operational services in support of NOAA's ongoing climate and fisheries research programs. The breadth of at-sea and shore-based activities, including hydrographic surveying, ecosystem sampling, instrument calibration, data processing, and vessel logistics coordination, requires the contractor to maintain a core team of not less than fourteen (14) qualified professionals throughout all active survey seasons. At a minimum, the proposed team shall include a Program Director with demonstrated oceanographic operations experience, two (2) Senior Marine Scientists, a Chief Survey Technician, four (4) junior survey technicians, a Data Management Lead, two (2) data analysts, a Vessel Logistics Coordinator, and at least two (2) field support specialists capable of deployment aboard NOAA research vessels. All personnel assigned to at-sea operations must possess current STCW Basic Safety Training certification prior to deployment. The offeror shall demonstrate in the Technical Approach volume that the proposed fourteen-person team possesses the collective expertise to support concurrent survey operations across multiple geographic regions without compromising data quality or regulatory compliance. The Government reserves the right to require the contractor to increase staffing levels during surge survey periods and will evaluate the offeror's scalability plan as part of the Management Approach assessment. Proposals that fail to demonstrate a committed team meeting the minimum fourteen-person threshold will not be evaluated further.

Contractors must comply with all Federal building security protocols, including identity verification and visitor management procedures enforced at the Madison location. Travel reimbursement to and from the Madison facility on a daily basis is not an allowable cost under this contract. The offeror shall demonstrate in their technical approach a plan for staffing qualified personnel within commutable distance of Madison, Wisconsin, or provide documentation of relocation plans for proposed key personnel prior to contract award. Example 2:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, Midwest Division, solicits offers for healthcare program integrity analysis services. The place of performance is CMS's Madison, Wisconsin regional analytical center. Analysts are required to perform all data processing and investigative review activities onsite at the Madison, WI facility due to data use agreement restrictions and HIPAA security requirements governing protected health information. No contractor-owned devices may be used; all work must be performed on government-furnished systems within the secure environment of the Madison location. Contractors must ensure proposed analysts possess the appropriate privacy training certifications prior to beginning onsite work in Madison, Wisconsin. The Government will provide a dedicated secure work area within the Madison facility for up to eight contractor personnel. Offerors shall address in their technical approach their plan for continuous onsite staffing at Madison, WI throughout the base period and all option periods, including contingency staffing procedures.

Example 3:
PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT – SECTION 4: The Department of Education's Office of Inspector General requires forensic audit and investigative support services. The primary place of performance is the OIG field office located in Madison, Wisconsin. Selected contractor personnel must be onsite at the Madison, WI office whenever conducting investigative interviews, reviewing grant documentation, or preparing case files for prosecutorial referral. Given the law enforcement sensitive nature of materials handled in this contract, no work product may be generated or stored outside of the Madison facility. The contractor shall assign a Madison-based site lead who is physically present onsite in Madison, Wisconsin no fewer than four days per week. All other assigned investigators must be available for onsite deployment to the Madison, WI location within twenty-four hours of notification. Offerors must identify in their staffing plan the residential locations of proposed personnel relative to Madison and confirm onsite availability commitments.

NATIONAL AERONAUTICS AND SPACE ADMINISTRATION
Glenn Research Center
Solicitation: NNC24ZCQ012R
Requirement: IT Infrastructure Maintenance and Support

THIS IS A HUBZONE SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE. Offers will only be considered from certified HUBZone small business concerns as defined in 13 C.F.R. § 126.103. The Government's decision to set aside this requirement is based on market research indicating sufficient HUBZone small business interest and capability to meet the requirement. Clause FAR 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole Source Award, applies to this solicitation. The applicable size standard under NAICS 541513 is $34.0 million in annual receipts. Offerors must confirm HUBZone certification via the SBA DUNS verification system prior to the closing date of this solicitation. NASA will review each offeror's certification status independently through SBA records. Firms whose HUBZone certification lapses between the date of offer and the date of award will be ineligible for contract award. HUBZone subcontracting arrangements do not satisfy the prime contractor certification requirement. Questions regarding HUBZone eligibility determinations must be submitted to the SBA, not to the NASA Contracting Officer.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE
Forest Service — Acquisition Management
Solicitation No. AG-84S8-S-24-0018
Project: Timber Stand Improvement and Reforestation Services

SET-ASIDE NOTICE: This acquisition is set aside 100% for HUBZone small business concerns in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.13. The Forest Service has determined, based on thorough market research, that this requirement meets the criteria for a HUBZone small business set-aside. Prospective offerors must be certified HUBZone small business concerns at the time proposals are submitted and must remain certified through the date of award. Certification must be current and reflected in both the SBA HUBZone database and SAM.gov at proposal closing. NAICS code 113310 applies with a size standard of $47.0 million in annual receipts. The solicitation incorporates FAR 52.219-3 by reference. All offerors must complete the HUBZone small business representation in their offer. Performance will occur in the Tongass National Forest, Alaska. The HUBZone certification requirement is non-waivable; no exceptions will be granted. The Government anticipates awarding multiple blanket purchase agreements to qualified HUBZone small businesses under this solicitation.

Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY
National Energy Technology Laboratory
Solicitation Reference: DE-SOL-0010988
Scope: Mechanical Engineering and Technical Support Services

NOTICE OF HUBZone SET-ASIDE: In accordance with FAR Subpart 19.13 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a, this solicitation is being conducted as a competitive HUBZone small business set-aside. The Department of Energy has determined that this requirement is appropriate for the HUBZone program based on current market research indicating the availability of multiple qualified HUBZone small business concerns capable of fulfilling the requirement at fair and reasonable prices. To qualify for this set-aside, firms must be certified as HUBZone small business concerns by the U.S. Small Business Administration. The HUBZone certification must be active and in good standing at the time of proposal submission. NAICS code 541330 (Engineering Services) applies with a size standard of $25.5 million. The Government will independently verify HUBZone certification status through SBA records. FAR 52.219-3 is included in this solicitation. Offerors must also comply with the HUBZone performance of work requirement, ensuring that at least 50% of the contract's labor cost is performed by HUBZone employees. The contract is anticipated to be a cost-plus-fixed-fee type with a period of performance of five years.

Forest Service — Pacific Southwest Region
Combined Synopsis/Solicitation
Solicitation Number: AG-82T7-S-24-0118

This is a combined synopsis/solicitation for commercial items prepared in accordance with the format in FAR Subpart 12.6 as supplemented with additional information included in this notice. SET-ASIDE: This acquisition is set aside exclusively for HUBZone small business concerns. In accordance with FAR 19.1305 and the implementing regulations at 13 C.F.R. The applicable NAICS code is 115310 (Support Activities for Forestry), with a size standard of $34.0 million. Work will be performed across multiple task order locations, and contractors must be prepared to mobilize throughout the region.

Offers must include a copy of the firm's current SBA HUBZone certification. The Government will not award to any firm that cannot affirmatively demonstrate HUBZone status at the time of offer and award. Award will be made on a best-value basis considering technical approach, past performance, and price.

Example 1:
Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

Example 2:
This solicitation for Healthcare Information Technology Modernization Support Services includes an oral presentation requirement as a formal element of the evaluation process. The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) will invite offerors in the competitive range to participate in structured oral presentations following the initial written proposal review. Oral presentations will be assessed under Technical Factor 2 – Implementation Methodology and Technical Factor 4 – Relevant Experience. Each offeror will have seventy-five (75) minutes to present, followed by a twenty-five (25) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the HHS Evaluation Committee. The offeror's proposed Program Manager, Lead Systems Architect, and at least one clinical informatics specialist must be present in person. Remote participation is not authorized except under emergency circumstances approved in advance by the Contracting Officer. Offerors should structure their oral presentation to directly address the evaluation criteria outlined in Section M and are advised that new information introduced during the oral presentation that was not contained in the written proposal will be evaluated as a clarification and not as a revision or amendment to the offer.

Example 3:
Offerors in the competitive range shall be required to participate in an oral presentation as part of the evaluation process. The oral presentation will be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Network Contracting Office and will be scheduled at the Government's discretion. The purpose of the oral presentation is to assess each offeror's understanding of the Performance Work Statement requirements, proposed staffing model, and quality control methodology. Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. All slide materials or visual aids must be provided to the Contracting Officer no later than three (3) business days prior to the presentation. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

The Department of Homeland Security, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, intends to procure professional software engineering services to support the development of a centralized threat intelligence aggregation and dissemination platform serving federal civilian executive branch agencies. The anticipated contract structure is a hybrid Time-and-Materials and Firm Fixed Price arrangement covering discrete development sprints and defined milestone deliverables respectively, with an estimated contract value of $31,750,000 over a four-year ordering period. The platform will ingest structured and unstructured threat data from multiple classified and unclassified sources, apply automated enrichment and correlation logic, and provide tailored intelligence products to agency subscribers through a secure API gateway and web portal. Contractors must demonstrate prior experience developing software systems that handle sensitive government information at the Controlled Unclassified Information level and above, and must maintain a facility clearance at the Secret level with personnel clearances commensurate with data access requirements. Technical requirements include implementation of STIX/TAXII standards for threat data exchange, integration with existing EINSTEIN network monitoring infrastructure, and support for machine-readable indicator sharing at machine speed. The contractor shall establish a program management office capable of coordinating across multiple CISA program teams and shall deliver monthly progress reports, a configuration management plan, and a system security plan in accordance with DHS 4300A Sensitive Systems Policy. Source selection will consider Technical Approach, Relevant Experience, Past Performance, and Price under a tradeoff methodology.

The General Services Administration Office of Technology Policy is issuing this Request for Proposal for a cloud-based contract writing and acquisition management platform. This procurement is strictly limited to the acquisition of an existing commercial solution. No software development is authorized under any circumstances. Offerors shall not propose to build, code, or engineer new application modules in response to agency-specific requirements. The Government's intent is to leverage a proven, market-available product and adapt internal business processes to align with the tool's existing capabilities. Configuration of user permissions, workflow routing, document templates, and integration with existing financial systems via standard APIs is permissible and expected. Offerors must demonstrate that the proposed platform has been deployed in at least three federal agency environments. Proposals that include statements of work referencing sprint cycles, agile development backlogs, or custom feature engineering will be evaluated unfavorably and may be deemed non-compliant with this solicitation's scope restrictions.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy Office of Environmental Management hereby solicits proposals for a project controls and earned value management system to support cleanup program oversight at multiple national laboratory sites. This acquisition is structured as a commercial item purchase under FAR Subpart 12.3 and requires a COTS solution required to be delivered without modification to core application logic. No software development is permitted under this contract. Offerors who propose to build custom modules, develop proprietary integrations requiring new code, or enhance existing product functionality through engineering effort will be found non-compliant. The permissible scope of work is configuration only and includes definition of work breakdown structures, cost account mapping, schedule baseline setup, earned value calculation method selection using product-native options, and report layout customization within the platform's standard reporting environment. The DOE contracting officer's representative will serve as the primary configuration authority and must approve all system settings prior to go-live. A configuration management plan shall be submitted within thirty days of contract award.

Example 3:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

This solicitation is issued under the express understanding that the Government intends to acquire a COTS solution required to satisfy all functional objectives identified in Section C of this document. FTA will not authorize the expenditure of contract funds for any form of custom software development, and Offerors should not anticipate receiving direction to build, modify, or extend application source code on behalf of the Government. DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION
FEDERAL HIGHWAY ADMINISTRATION
SOLICITATION NUMBER: DTFH6124R00031

SET-ASIDE: Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB)

The Federal Highway Administration (FHWA), Office of Infrastructure, is issuing this Request for Proposals as a total SDVOSB set-aside in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.14. This solicitation covers environmental compliance monitoring and reporting services along designated federal highway corridors in the Rocky Mountain Region. NAICS code 541620 (Environmental Consulting Services) applies with a size standard of $19 million. Only firms that qualify as Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Businesses under FAR 2.101 and 13 CFR Part 128 may submit proposals. The contracting officer has reviewed available market sources and determined that at least two or more SDVOSB concerns are capable of performing this work at competitive prices. Offerors shall provide documentation confirming SDVOSB status, including copies of relevant disability ratings and ownership certifications, with their technical proposal submissions. FHWA reserves the right to cancel the SDVOSB set-aside designation and re-solicit under a different socioeconomic category should adequate competition not materialize.

Example 2:
SMALL BUSINESS ADMINISTRATION
DISTRICT OPERATIONS SUPPORT
RFP NUMBER: SBA-2024-DC-0053

SET-ASIDE DETERMINATION: Pursuant to Federal Acquisition Regulation Part 19.1405 and FAR clause 52.219-27, this solicitation is designated as a 100% Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) set-aside. The U.S. Small Business Administration, Office of Administrative Services, is seeking administrative and clerical support staffing services for SBA district offices in the Mid-Atlantic region, including offices located in Maryland, Virginia, Delaware, and the District of Columbia. The applicable NAICS code is 561110 (Office Administrative Services) with a size standard of $12 million. Only offerors that certify their status as a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business in SAM.gov and comply with the ownership and control requirements of 13 CFR Part 128 are eligible to receive an award under this solicitation. In evaluating offers, the contracting officer will verify SDVOSB certifications prior to any award decision. Concerns that cannot demonstrate compliance with the definition of a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business as stated in FAR 2.101 will be found ineligible for award. The government anticipates awarding a single-award time-and-materials task order contract with a one-year base period and up to three one-year option periods.

Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS
VETERANS HEALTH ADMINISTRATION
SOLICITATION: 36C10X24R0221

PURCHASE TYPE: This requirement is a TOTAL SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESS (SDVOSB) SET-ASIDE pursuant to 38 U.S.C. 8127 and 38 CFR Part 74, as implemented by VAAR 819.7005. The Veterans Health Administration (VHA), Network Contracting Office 20, is procuring laundry and linen management services for the VA Puget Sound Health Care System, Seattle Division and American Lake Division. Services include collection, laundering, pressing, folding, and return delivery of patient linens, surgical textiles, and staff uniforms on a recurring basis. NAICS code 812320 (Drycleaning and Laundry Services, except Coin-Operated) applies with a size standard of $8 million. Pursuant to 38 U.S.C. 8127(d), a contract may be awarded only to a verified Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business that is listed in the VA Vendor Information Pages (VIP) database as verified. Offerors must maintain VIP verification status through the date of award. Award will be on a best-value tradeoff basis considering technical approach, past performance, and price. Estimated contract value is between $500,000 and $2,000,000 over the full period of performance.

RFP chunk to analyze:
SECTION B – CONTRACT TYPE AND ELIGIBILITY

This acquisition is being conducted as a Full and Open Competition pursuant to FAR Part 6.3. All responsible sources may submit a proposal. The contracting officer has reviewed applicable set-aside programs, including the Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) program administered through the Small Business Administration, and has determined that sufficient competition cannot be achieved through a restricted set-aside at this time. Market research conducted in accordance with FAR Part 10 indicated fewer than two verified SDVOSB entities capable of fulfilling the full scope of the Performance Work Statement. Accordingly, this solicitation is issued on an unrestricted basis. Offerors are advised that participation by SDVOSBs, veteran-owned small businesses, and other small business categories is encouraged and will be considered favorably under the agency's small business subcontracting goals. Prime contractors awarded this contract with a total value exceeding $750,000 will be required to submit a Small Business Subcontracting Plan identifying opportunities for SDVOSB and other small business participation at the subcontract level. However, no portion of this solicitation constitutes a set-aside for any specific socioeconomic category. All evaluation criteria apply uniformly to all offerors regardless of business size or ownership status.

Example 1:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Southwestern Division, hereby issues this solicitation for SharePoint-based knowledge management system services. The Division has standardized on Microsoft SharePoint Online as its enterprise collaboration platform under the Army's existing M365 enterprise agreement and requires a contractor to configure and deploy a structured knowledge repository supporting engineering project documentation, lessons learned capture, and cross-district information sharing. The contractor shall design and implement a SharePoint site collection hierarchy, configure content types, managed metadata term stores, search schema, and retention policies consistent with Army Records Management requirements. The Government does not require and will not fund proprietary software development under this task order. The contractor shall provide all configuration through SharePoint's native administrative interfaces and approved Power Platform tools. Training shall be provided for up to 60 knowledge managers across six district offices. Final deliverables include a fully operational configured SharePoint environment and comprehensive administrator and end-user documentation.

Example 2:
The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Office of Education invites qualified vendors to submit proposals for the redesign and deployment of a public educational outreach website. NOAA has determined that a pre-built platform such as Squarespace or an equivalent commercially available website builder satisfies the functional needs of this effort, which is primarily informational in nature and does not require custom application development or backend database integration. The contractor shall configure and deploy the selected commercial platform, ensuring full compliance with NOAA digital branding standards, Section 508 accessibility requirements, and applicable federal web policy. Deliverables include site architecture documentation, configured and populated site with a minimum of 50 content pages, staff training for designated content editors, and a 12-month support retainer for platform administration. Proposals shall identify the specific commercial off-the-shelf platform proposed, justify its selection against the functional requirements in Section C, and provide a fixed-price implementation schedule.

Example 3:
The Department of the Interior, Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA), issues this solicitation for website design and content management services for the BIA Office of Trust Services public information portal. The Government intends to deploy a commercial off-the-shelf content management platform, specifically Wix Enterprise or an equivalent commercially available solution, to support public-facing informational content delivery without incurring custom software development costs. The contractor shall configure and deploy the selected commercial platform, develop site templates consistent with USA.gov design standards and BIA branding guidelines, populate initial content provided by the BIA communications team, and establish a content governance workflow for ongoing updates. Proposals must address how the proposed pre-built platform solution achieves compliance with Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act and WCAG 2.1 Level AA standards. The contractor shall provide a minimum of eight hours of training for designated BIA content administrators and shall remain available for platform administration support during the 12-month base period and two option years.

RFP chunk to analyze:
This acquisition supports the Office of Research and Evaluation's longitudinal study infrastructure requirements, specifically the need for a purpose-engineered participant tracking and survey instrumentation system capable of managing cohort data across a fifteen-year study horizon. The system must be engineered to accommodate evolving data collection instruments, support complex skip-logic and adaptive branching in survey protocols, and maintain a complete, immutable audit trail of all data modifications in accordance with 21 CFR Part 11 requirements. The contractor shall build all application components to agency specifications, including custom participant identity resolution logic, researcher-configurable instrument authoring tools, and a longitudinal data linkage engine. The Government is aware that Salesforce and similar customer relationship management platforms are marketed for use in research participant management contexts; these platforms were assessed and found unsuitable because they do not natively support the required audit immutability standards, cannot be configured to meet the agency's data residency requirements without substantial architectural compromise, and introduce contractual terms incompatible with the Government's data ownership policies. No commercial CRM, survey platform, or research management product shall serve as a foundational layer in the proposed solution architecture. The contractor assumes full responsibility for original software design and implementation throughout the period of performance.

NOTICE OF TOTAL SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE: Pursuant to the Small Business Act and Federal Acquisition Regulation Part 19, this solicitation is set aside exclusively for small business concerns. The Department of Transportation, Federal Highway Administration, is seeking qualified small business vendors to provide pavement condition assessment and highway infrastructure inspection services. NAICS Code 541330 applies with a size standard of $19.0 million. The two-in-ten rule has been applied and market research confirms the availability of two or more responsible small business concerns capable of performing this work at fair market prices. Offers submitted by large businesses will not be evaluated and shall be considered nonresponsive. By submission of an offer, the offeror represents that it is a small business concern under the size standard associated with the above NAICS code. Example 2:
ACQUISITION TYPE: Total Small Business Set-Aside. The Department of Justice, Bureau of Prisons, announces this competitive procurement restricted exclusively to small business concerns. The required services encompass medical and dental staffing support for federal correctional institutions in the Southeast Region. The applicable NAICS code is 561320 (Temporary Help Services), with a size standard of $34.0 million in average annual receipts. This set-aside is established under the authority of FAR 19.502-2 and FAR 52.219-6. By submission of a proposal, each offeror certifies that it is a small business concern under the applicable size standard. Offerors must be registered in SAM.gov with an active and current small business self-certification prior to solicitation closing. In accordance with FAR 52.219-6, only offers from small business concerns, as defined under 13 C.F.R. Part 121, will be accepted and evaluated. Example 1:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Example 2:
In accordance with the Department of Homeland Security's Digital Services Strategy, all software engineering work performed under this contract shall follow Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) practices. The contractor shall conduct Program Increment (PI) Planning events every ten weeks, bringing together all Agile Release Train teams to align on objectives and identify dependencies. Each Agile team shall operate in two-week iterations with clearly defined iteration goals established during iteration planning ceremonies. The contractor shall designate Release Train Engineers to facilitate ART-level events and support teams in removing cross-team impediments. The System Demo, conducted at the end of each PI, shall be open to all Government stakeholders and demonstrate integrated working software. The contractor shall maintain and update Team and Program backlogs within the Government's approved Agile lifecycle management tooling. Inspect and Adapt workshops shall be conducted at the close of each PI to drive continuous improvement and quantify the improvement backlog.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

This solicitation is issued pursuant to FAR Part 12 and FAR Part 15 for the acquisition of professional services in support of the Department's strategic planning and organizational transformation initiatives. The scope of work includes facilitation of executive workshops, development of a five-year strategic roadmap, and production of an implementation plan with measurable performance indicators aligned to the agency's strategic goals. Transformation planning engagements in the private sector frequently employ sprint-style working sessions as a workshop facilitation technique; however, this reference to sprints describes a time-boxed meeting format commonly used in consulting practice and should not be construed as a requirement for the contractor to operate under any software development framework. The deliverables under this contract are analytical and advisory in nature and include a Current State Assessment Report, a Future State Vision Document, a Gap Analysis, and a Prioritized Implementation Roadmap. These deliverables are due on fixed dates specified in the Period of Performance schedule. The contractor's internal project management approach is at their discretion, provided deliverable quality standards and submission deadlines established in Section F are met.

Example 1:
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration solicits proposals for advanced telemetry processing software and, as part of this acquisition, mandates a design challenge for all offerors in the competitive range. The design challenge will require offeror teams to receive a raw telemetry data stream, process it using their proposed software architecture, and produce a formatted output file meeting specifications outlined in Appendix F within a two-hour window. NASA evaluators will monitor all aspects of the exercise and score submissions based on data fidelity, processing speed, error handling, and output compliance. Offerors may use their own laptops and licensed software tools but may not access external networks during the exercise. The design challenge is specifically intended to assess whether proposed technical approaches translate into functional capability under realistic mission constraints. Results of the design challenge will be incorporated into the Mission Suitability technical factor score and weighted at twenty-five percent (25%) of that factor. NASA reserves the right to modify the complexity of the telemetry dataset to ensure differentiation among offerors.

Example 2:
In accordance with the evaluation methodology described herein, the Department of Veterans Affairs requires each offeror to deliver a prototype submission of their proposed electronic health record integration module. The prototype submission shall demonstrate bidirectional data exchange between the offeror's platform and a VA-supplied test instance of the existing legacy system. Offerors must configure and host the prototype in a government-authorized cloud environment prior to the evaluation period. VA technical evaluators will conduct a structured review session lasting no more than three (3) hours, during which they will execute pre-defined integration test scripts and evaluate system responses. The prototype submission will be used to validate claims made in the written technical proposal; any discrepancies between the written proposal and demonstrated prototype functionality may result in a downward adjustment to the technical rating. Offerors must submit prototype access credentials to the Contracting Officer no fewer than five (5) business days before the scheduled evaluation session. Detailed instructions for the VA test environment configuration are provided in Section J, Attachment 7.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration hereby issues this solicitation which includes a required design exercise component for all competitive range offerors. Specifically, each offeror must submit a prototype submission demonstrating their proposed citizen-facing web portal solution. The prototype submission shall be a fully navigable, hosted demonstration environment accessible via government-provided credentials. The prototype must include functional user registration, document upload, and status tracking modules. GSA evaluators will engage directly with the prototype during a structured two-hour evaluation session and will simulate end-user workflows as described in Attachment D, User Scenario Matrix. Prototype submissions are due no later than fourteen (14) days prior to the scheduled oral presentations. Offerors are advised that the prototype submission will be evaluated independently from the written technical volume and carries a weight of thirty-five percent (35%) of the total technical score. Any prototype that is unavailable, incomplete, or non-functional during the evaluation session will receive a rating of Unacceptable for that factor.

The U.S. Department of Energy, Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE), is soliciting proposals for the development of next-generation grid-scale battery management software. As part of this competitive solicitation, all offerors must submit a functional prototype demonstrating their proposed software platform no later than forty-five (45) calendar days following solicitation release. The prototype submission shall be hosted in a vendor-managed cloud environment accessible to government evaluators through EERE-issued credentials. At minimum, the prototype must demonstrate real-time battery state-of-charge monitoring, predictive degradation modeling, and automated charge cycle optimization across a simulated array of no fewer than one hundred (100) virtual battery nodes. Evaluation of the prototype submission will be conducted during a two-hour structured demonstration session facilitated by EERE's technical evaluation panel. Offerors will be expected to narrate system functionality, respond to evaluator-directed scenario injections, and perform live configuration adjustments during the session. The prototype submission factor carries a weight of forty percent (40%) of the total Technical Evaluation score. Offerors are strongly encouraged to review the Prototype Evaluation Rubric included as Attachment F prior to initiating development. A prototype that is inaccessible, non-functional, or unable to complete the required demonstration scenarios at the time of the evaluation session will be deemed Unacceptable. EERE reserves the right to extend the prototype submission deadline by written amendment should the solicitation timeline be adjusted.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

Example 2:
The Social Security Administration requires all IT development work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement to comply with SSA's Agile Development Standards. Offerors shall describe their Agile delivery model in the technical approach and demonstrate how their proposed methodology aligns with Scrum or Kanban frameworks. Contractors shall conduct sprint planning meetings with SSA's Product Owner to decompose epics into sprint-ready user stories with acceptance criteria no later than the iteration planning session preceding each sprint. The contractor shall update the Government's enterprise project management tool daily with sprint board status, impediments, and burndown data. Demonstration of working software to SSA stakeholders shall occur at the end of every sprint, and the Government reserves the right to redirect backlog priorities following each sprint review based on mission needs. The contractor shall implement Definition of Ready criteria to ensure user stories are sufficiently detailed prior to sprint commitment. Quarterly business reviews shall include Agile performance metrics, including average sprint velocity, defect density, and sprint goal completion percentage.

Example 3:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Section C.4 – Development Methodology and Execution Standards. The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires the contractor to employ Agile Scrum as the sole software development methodology for all work performed under this Performance Work Statement. This requirement reflects the Agency's commitment to iterative delivery, early risk reduction, and continuous stakeholder engagement throughout the software development lifecycle. The contractor shall organize all development staff into stable, dedicated Scrum teams of no fewer than five and no more than nine members. A certified Scrum Master, holding a recognized certification such as CSM, PSM I, or equivalent, shall be assigned to each team and shall serve as a servant-leader responsible for Agile ceremony facilitation, impediment removal, and coaching team members on Agile practices. Sprint length shall be fixed at two weeks and shall not be modified without written approval from the Contracting Officer. Sprint planning shall establish a clearly defined sprint goal and a committed sprint backlog drawn from the prioritized product backlog. The contractor shall maintain a transparent, accessible product backlog within the Government's designated toolset and shall conduct backlog refinement activities no less than once per sprint. Sprint review meetings shall include demonstration of completed, tested, and accepted user stories to the Government Product Owner and other designated stakeholders. The contractor shall produce and deliver Agile performance metrics including velocity trends, defect escape rates, and team capacity utilization as attachments to the monthly progress report submitted to the Contracting Officer's Representative.

Example 1:
Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) intends to conduct oral presentations with offerors as part of its evaluation of proposals submitted in response to this solicitation for Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Support Services. Oral presentations will be conducted following the initial screening of written proposals. Only those offerors determined to be technically acceptable after written review will be scheduled for an oral presentation. The oral presentation will focus primarily on the offeror's technical methodology, data management approach, and the qualifications of proposed scientific personnel. Each session is limited to one hundred and twenty (120) minutes, including a thirty (30) minute question period. The proposed Principal Investigator must personally attend and lead the oral presentation. Additional team members may participate at the offeror's discretion but are subject to a five-person maximum. Materials presented must not introduce new technical approaches not previously described in the written proposal, and any inconsistencies identified will be evaluated unfavorably.

Example 3:
Section M – Evaluation Criteria: The Department of Homeland Security will evaluate all responsive proposals using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology. Following written proposal evaluation, offerors deemed within the competitive range will be invited to deliver an oral presentation to the Source Selection Evaluation Board (SSEB). The oral presentation is intended to allow offerors to clarify and elaborate upon their written technical submissions and to demonstrate the depth of understanding of the program requirements. Presentations shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. The lead proposed Program Manager and at least two key technical personnel identified in the proposal must participate in person. Remote participation via video teleconference will be permitted only upon written approval by the Contracting Officer. Scores assigned during the oral presentation phase will be integrated with written proposal scores to produce a final technical rating for each offeror.

CMS is committed to a commercial-first technology strategy and accordingly requires that all proposed solutions be fully developed, commercially available products at the time of proposal submission. No custom software development will be authorized or funded under the resulting contract. Vendors shall not propose to develop new features, build custom application layers, or write integration code specific to CMS environments. Configuration only activities are within scope and shall include case queue setup, escalation rule definition, knowledge base population, and API-based integration with existing CMS data systems using pre-built connectors provided by the software vendor. The Government anticipates that some desired capabilities may require process adaptation rather than system customization, and offerors should address this expectation directly in their technical approach. Proposals that substitute development commitments for existing product functionality will receive reduced technical scores.

Example 2:
The Department of Justice Office of Justice Programs is issuing this solicitation for a performance measurement and reporting platform to support grant recipient monitoring activities. OJP requires a commercially available product with demonstrated use in federal grant management contexts. This is not a software development contract. No custom development of any application component is within scope, and proposals containing such elements will be rejected. The Government's intent is to configure an existing platform to capture grantee performance data, generate compliance reports, and provide dashboard visualizations using tools native to the commercial product. Configuration-only work is authorized, including form design using the platform's built-in form builder, workflow definition using the platform's process configuration module, and user access management. The Contractor shall provide a configuration workbook documenting all settings applied during implementation. OJP will retain the ability to perform ongoing configuration adjustments using administrative tools provided within the product. No source code access, compiled code deliverables, or software release packages are expected or permitted under this contract.

Example 3:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

FSA requires the contractor to employ a waterfall development lifecycle consistent with USDA's Departmental Regulation 3300-001 governing enterprise application development. The contractor shall organize all development activities into the following sequential phases: Requirements Elicitation and Validation, System Architecture and Design, Database Design, Application Development, Unit and Integration Testing, System Testing, User Acceptance Testing, and Production Implementation. Formal phase gate reviews shall be conducted at the completion of each phase, and the Government's written approval to proceed is a prerequisite for initiation of any subsequent phase. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established upon Government acceptance of the Approved System Requirements Document. Modifications to the approved baseline are subject to the FSA Change Management process, require documented business justification, and must receive written Contracting Officer authorization before implementation.

Example 2:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

Example 3:

In accordance with GSA Order CIO 2100.1, the contractor shall utilize standard software lifecycle development practices as the governing development framework for this engagement. The project shall be organized into five sequential phases: Requirements Gathering, Architecture and Design, Development, Testing and Quality Assurance, and Deployment and Transition. Phase-to-phase progression is contingent upon successful completion of formal Government review and written authorization to proceed. The contractor is expressly prohibited from initiating coding activities prior to approval of the Functional Requirements Document and System Architecture Document. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. Subsequent changes to baselined requirements must be processed through the formal Engineering Change Proposal process and approved in writing by the Contracting Officer.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

Forest Service — Acquisition Management

Prospective offerors must be certified HUBZone small business concerns at the time proposals are submitted and must remain certified through the date of award. Certification must be current and reflected in both the SBA HUBZone database and SAM.gov at proposal closing. NAICS code 113310 applies with a size standard of $47.0 million in annual receipts. The solicitation incorporates FAR 52.219-3 by reference. All offerors must complete the HUBZone small business representation in their offer. Performance will occur in the Tongass National Forest, Alaska. The HUBZone certification requirement is non-waivable; no exceptions will be granted. The Government anticipates awarding multiple blanket purchase agreements to qualified HUBZone small businesses under this solicitation.

The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

The contractor shall execute the project through clearly delineated sequential phases: Concept of Operations, Requirements Definition, Preliminary Design, Critical Design, Code and Unit Test, Integration and Test, System Acceptance Test, and Operational Deployment. A formal phase gate review shall be conducted at the conclusion of each phase, with Government approval required before work proceeds. Requirements established at the conclusion of the Requirements Definition phase shall constitute the fixed requirements baseline and shall be placed under configuration management control. No development work shall begin prior to Government approval of the System Requirements Specification document.

RFP chunk to analyze:
The contractor shall maintain a program-level roadmap updated no less than quarterly, reflecting current priorities, anticipated capability releases, and dependency mapping across workstreams. This roadmap is expressly not a Gantt chart depicting sequential phases with hard gate criteria; it is a probabilistic planning artifact subject to continuous refinement as new information emerges from user testing and stakeholder engagement. The distinction is operationally significant: unlike standard software lifecycle development approaches that treat planning artifacts as contractually binding schedules, the roadmap under this Task Order functions as a communication and coordination tool rather than a performance baseline. Earned value reporting shall be adapted to reflect agile metrics including planned versus completed story points, release burndown, and cumulative flow diagrams. Where agency financial systems require mapping to a work breakdown structure, the contractor shall collaborate with the Contracting Officer's Representative to establish a WBS that accommodates iterative delivery without artificially imposing phase-sequential logic. Deviations from the roadmap that reflect reprioritization decisions made jointly by the Product Owner and contractor do not constitute schedule variances requiring corrective action plans.

In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

Example 2:
Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

Example 3:
As part of the evaluation methodology for this Information Technology Services procurement, the General Services Administration (GSA) will incorporate oral presentations into the source selection process. After an initial review of written proposals, offerors receiving a rating of Acceptable or higher under the Technical Factor will be invited to participate in an oral presentation before the Technical Evaluation Team. The oral presentation allows the Government to assess offeror capabilities beyond what is conveyed in written form and to evaluate proposed personnel directly. Each offeror will be allotted ninety (90) minutes total: sixty (60) minutes for the presentation and thirty (30) minutes for Government-directed questions. Key personnel named in Section H must be present and actively participate. Substitutions of key personnel for the oral presentation will not be permitted without prior written approval and may negatively impact the evaluation. Offerors should note that the oral presentation is evaluated separately under Factor 2 – Management Approach and scored on an Adjectival basis consistent with the criteria described in Section M.

Example 1:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services requires the contractor to apply Agile methodology across all workstreams supporting the modernization of legacy claims processing systems. The contractor shall organize work into user stories, epics, and themes within a hierarchical backlog structure and shall demonstrate traceability from individual user stories to program-level epics and strategic objectives. Sprints shall be conducted on a two-week cadence with sprint planning occurring on the first day of each sprint and sprint review and retrospective conducted on the final day. The Government's designated Product Owner shall have authority to accept or reject sprint deliverables based on the agreed Definition of Done. The contractor shall not carry incomplete user stories across sprint boundaries without documented justification and re-estimation. The contractor shall facilitate quarterly Agile maturity assessments using a Government-approved framework and shall present findings and improvement recommendations to the CMS program office. Test-Driven Development (TDD) and Continuous Integration practices shall be employed as integral components of the Agile engineering workflow.

Example 2:
Pursuant to the Department of Defense's software acquisition pathway as defined in DoDI 5000.87, the contractor shall deliver capabilities using Agile software development methodology with continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines. The contractor shall organize personnel into Agile squads operating in two-week sprints, with each squad responsible for a defined set of product backlog items. All Agile ceremonies, including sprint planning, daily standups, sprint reviews, and retrospectives, shall be conducted in person or via Government-approved collaboration tools. The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. A product roadmap updated at minimum quarterly shall be maintained and shared with Government stakeholders to provide visibility into planned increments of capability. The use of Kanban boards or equivalent visual management tools is required to provide real-time transparency into work-in-progress, blocked items, and completed work. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

NOTICE OF TOTAL SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE: Pursuant to the Small Business Act and Federal Acquisition Regulation Part 19, this solicitation is set aside exclusively for small business concerns. The Department of Transportation, Federal Highway Administration, is seeking qualified small business vendors to provide pavement condition assessment and highway infrastructure inspection services. NAICS Code 541330 applies with a size standard of $19.0 million. The two-in-ten rule has been applied and market research confirms the availability of two or more responsible small business concerns capable of performing this work at fair market prices. Offers submitted by large businesses will not be evaluated and shall be considered nonresponsive. By submission of an offer, the offeror represents that it is a small business concern under the size standard associated with the above NAICS code. This set-aside determination is final and no requests for waiver will be entertained.

Example 2:
This contract action is a 100% Small Business Set-Aside. The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Management, solicits offers from small business concerns only for laboratory analytical services in support of Superfund site investigations. In accordance with FAR 52.219-6, Notice of Total Small Business Set-Aside, the Government will not consider offers from businesses that do not qualify as small under NAICS code 541380 (Testing Laboratories), size standard $19.0 million. Market research conducted by the Contracting Officer revealed a reasonable expectation that offers will be received from at least two responsible small businesses and that award can be made at fair and reasonable prices. Prospective offerors are advised that the nonmanufacturer rule and related regulations under FAR Part 19 apply to this procurement. All offerors must certify their small business status in SAM prior to the solicitation closing date.

Example 3:
This acquisition is a TOTAL SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE in accordance with FAR 19.502-2. Only offers from small business concerns will be considered for award. The applicable North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code is 541611, with a corresponding size standard of $16.5 million in average annual receipts. The Department of Veterans Affairs, Office of Acquisition Operations, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for management consulting services to support the Veterans Benefits Administration. Any offer received from other than a small business concern shall be rejected. Offerors must represent that they qualify as a small business under the applicable NAICS code at the time of offer submission and at the time of award. Registration and small business representation in the System for Award Management (SAM) at www.sam.gov is required prior to award. The Government reserves the right to cancel this solicitation if it is determined that small business competition is insufficient to ensure fair and reasonable pricing.

NOTICE TO OFFERORS — SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE: The Department of Health and Human Services, Indian Health Service, Office of Management Services, announces that this solicitation for information technology staffing and helpdesk support services is designated as a 100% Small Business Set-Aside pursuant to FAR 19.502-2. Only offers submitted by concerns that qualify as small businesses under NAICS code 541519 (Other Computer Related Services), size standard $34.0 million in average annual receipts, will be considered for award. This determination was made following a comprehensive market analysis that confirmed the availability of multiple responsible small business concerns with demonstrated capability to perform the scope of work described herein. Large business offerors are expressly prohibited from submitting proposals under this solicitation, and any such submission received will be rejected without further consideration. Offerors must affirmatively represent their small business size status in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) no later than the closing date of this solicitation. Misrepresentation of size status is a violation of federal law and may result in criminal prosecution, civil penalties, and debarment from future federal contracting. The Indian Health Service reserves the right to refer any size status concern to the Small Business Administration for a formal size determination. The anticipated contract type is a time-and-materials contract with a 12-month base period and up to four 12-month option periods. The total estimated value of this contract, inclusive of all option periods, is approximately $8,200,000. Offerors should note that the limitations on subcontracting clause at FAR 52.219-14 requires the prime contractor to perform at least 50 percent of the labor hours with its own personnel.
