Example 1:
SOLICITATION NUMBER: HHS-2024-EDWOSB-0042

This acquisition is a 100% set-aside for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns under the authority of 15 U.S.C. 637(m). The Department of Health and Human Services, Office of Acquisition Services, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for the provision of professional health information technology consulting services in support of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services modernization initiative. Only offerors who are certified Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns at the time of offer submission and at the time of award are eligible to receive this contract. Offerors must be registered in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) and must have a current EDWOSB certification through the SBA certification program or a third-party certifier approved by the Small Business Administration. The applicable NAICS code is 541512 with a small business size standard of $34 million in average annual receipts. Any offer received from a concern that does not qualify as an EDWOSB at the time of offer will be considered nonresponsive and will not be evaluated. Questions regarding EDWOSB eligibility requirements should be directed to the contracting officer prior to the closing date of this solicitation.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE
AGRICULTURAL RESEARCH SERVICE
Solicitation Number: 12-25-B-0034

This solicitation is being conducted as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.15 and 13 CFR Part 127. The USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) requires logistics, warehousing, and distribution services in support of research specimen and laboratory supply chain operations at the National Centers for Animal Health complex in Ames, Iowa. The applicable NAICS code for this requirement is 493110 – General Warehousing and Storage Facilities, and the applicable size standard is $34 million in average annual receipts. Pursuant to the EDWOSB set-aside designation, only offers submitted by firms that are currently certified as Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns will be considered. To be considered economically disadvantaged, the woman or women who own and control the firm must meet the personal financial criteria established at 13 CFR 127.200, including limitations on personal net worth, adjusted gross income, and total assets. Offerors should include their SBA-issued EDWOSB certification number or third-party certifier documentation in Volume I of their offer. Awards made under this solicitation are subject to the limitations on subcontracting requirements applicable to EDWOSB set-asides as specified in FAR 52.219-30.

Example 3:
GENERAL SERVICES ADMINISTRATION
FEDERAL ACQUISITION SERVICE
Solicitation Reference: GSA-FAS-2024-0763

This notice serves as a combined synopsis/solicitation for commercial items prepared in accordance with the format in FAR Subpart 12.6, as supplemented with additional information included in this notice. The Government intends to award a contract on an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside basis for janitorial and facility maintenance services at the federally owned building located at 400 Maryland Avenue SW, Washington, DC. The applicable North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code is 561720 – Building Cleaning Services, with a corresponding size standard of $22 million in average annual receipts. Pursuant to 13 CFR Part 127, only firms that have been certified as Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Businesses by the SBA or an approved third-party certifier are eligible to submit offers in response to this solicitation. Offerors must provide evidence of their EDWOSB certification along with their technical proposal. The period of performance will be a 12-month base period with four 12-month option periods. The Government reserves the right to cancel this solicitation if adequate EDWOSB competition is not obtained.

Public Buildings Service
Request for Proposals — Janitorial and Custodial Services
Solicitation: 47PA0124R0012

SET-ASIDE STATUS: This solicitation is issued as a total HUBZone small business set-aside in accordance with Federal Acquisition Regulation 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole Source Award. All interested parties must be certified HUBZone small business concerns as defined in FAR 19.1301 and must appear as such in the SBA's HUBZone database at the time offers are due. Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY
National Energy Technology Laboratory
Solicitation Reference: DE-SOL-0010988
Scope: Mechanical Engineering and Technical Support Services

NOTICE OF HUBZone SET-ASIDE: In accordance with FAR Subpart 19.13 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a, this solicitation is being conducted as a competitive HUBZone small business set-aside. The Department of Energy has determined that this requirement is appropriate for the HUBZone program based on current market research indicating the availability of multiple qualified HUBZone small business concerns capable of fulfilling the requirement at fair and reasonable prices. To qualify for this set-aside, firms must be certified as HUBZone small business concerns by the U.S. Small Business Administration. The HUBZone certification must be active and in good standing at the time of proposal submission. NAICS code 541330 (Engineering Services) applies with a size standard of $25.5 million. The Government will independently verify HUBZone certification status through SBA records. FAR 52.219-3 is included in this solicitation. Offerors must also comply with the HUBZone performance of work requirement, ensuring that at least 50% of the contract's labor cost is performed by HUBZone employees. The contract is anticipated to be a cost-plus-fixed-fee type with a period of performance of five years.

Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION
Federal Highway Administration
Solicitation Number: DTFH6124R00034
Program: Transportation Planning and Data Analysis

AGENCY SET-ASIDE DETERMINATION:

After conducting market research in accordance with FAR Part 10, the Contracting Officer has determined that this requirement shall be set aside exclusively for HUBZone small business concerns. This HUBZone set-aside is established under the authority of FAR 19.1305 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a. To be eligible to receive award under this solicitation, offerors must (1) be certified as a HUBZone small business concern by the SBA, (2) be registered in SAM with an active HUBZone designation, and (3) continue to qualify as a HUBZone small business through the duration of contract performance. The NAICS code for this acquisition is 541490, with a size standard of $19.0 million. Offerors must include their SBA-issued HUBZone certification number in their proposal cover letter. The Government will not consider offers from firms that do not hold an active HUBZone certification. Estimated contract value is $2.4 million over a base year plus four option years.

The Department of Veterans Affairs, Office of Information and Technology, is seeking a qualified contractor to design, develop, test, and deploy a custom case management platform to support the Benefits Delivery at Discharge program. The selected contractor shall provide full software development lifecycle support, including requirements analysis, architecture design, iterative development, integration with existing VA enterprise systems, and ongoing maintenance. The platform shall enable real-time data exchange with the Defense Enrollment Eligibility Reporting System (DEERS) and support secure role-based access for VA adjudicators, veterans service representatives, and medical staff across regional offices. The contractor shall adhere to VA Technical Reference Model standards and ensure all deliverables comply with Section 508 accessibility requirements and FISMA Moderate security controls. The period of performance consists of a 12-month base period with two 12-month option periods. Contract type will be a Firm-Fixed-Price task order issued under an existing Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity vehicle. The government anticipates a team of four to seven full-time equivalent personnel including a project manager, software engineers, a DevSecOps engineer, and a quality assurance specialist. All work may be performed remotely subject to the contractor maintaining connectivity to VA development environments through VA-approved VPN infrastructure. Interested parties should note that source selection will be conducted using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology evaluating technical approach, past performance, and price, with technical factors considered more important than price. A draft Performance Work Statement will be released for industry comment prior to final solicitation issuance.

Example 1:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency (DLA), releases this solicitation for enterprise resource planning support services for the Enterprise Business System (EBS), DLA's primary supply chain management and financial management platform built on SAP. This effort involves the contractor taking over an existing system from the current support team and assuming responsibility for all Tier 2 and Tier 3 technical support, configuration management, and enhancement development activities. The existing system comprises a heavily customized SAP ECC 6.0 environment with over 900 custom ABAP objects developed and accumulated over more than a decade of prior contract performance. DLA requires offerors to demonstrate specific technical experience with transition from incumbent SAP support contracts, particularly where the incoming contractor was required to absorb undocumented customizations and reconstruct functional design documentation. The period of performance includes a mandatory 60-day parallel operations phase during which transition from the outgoing contractor will be completed under Government oversight.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of Federal Student Aid (FSA), announces a solicitation for continued development and operational support of the StudentAid.gov platform and its underlying data services. This contract represents continuing work on a platform that serves over 43 million borrowers and applicants annually. The selected contractor will receive a comprehensive handoff package from the incumbent contractor, including all source code repositories, infrastructure-as-code scripts, API documentation, and outstanding feature roadmap commitments. The incoming contractor must demonstrate the capacity to onboard into the existing system rapidly, with mission-critical services such as the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) processing pipeline remaining fully operational throughout transition. FSA's evaluation methodology includes an oral presentation component during which offerors will be asked to describe specific technical challenges encountered when taking over legacy codebases and the resolution approaches applied. Key personnel proposed for this effort must be available to begin transition activities within two weeks of contract award.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Acquisition Service seeks a contractor to provide software development and maintenance support for the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, is seeking proposals for the continued sustainment and enhancement of the Homeland Security Information Network (HSIN). This procurement represents a transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, whose period of performance under Contract Number HSHQDC-19-C-00041 concludes on March 31, 2025. The selected contractor will assume full operational responsibility for the existing system, which serves over 120,000 federal, state, local, tribal, and territorial users across 48 mission areas. The Government acknowledges that the legacy codebase contains components developed under multiple prior contracts spanning more than a decade, and offerors must demonstrate a systematic methodology for documenting and rationalizing inherited technical debt. A mandatory 120-day transition period will be established during which the outgoing and incoming contractors shall operate concurrently to ensure continuity of mission-critical collaboration services. The incoming contractor is expected to conduct a full inventory of the existing system's architecture, including all integrations with external agency systems, authentication services, and data feeds. Offerors must provide a Transition Risk Management Plan as a standalone attachment to the technical volume, addressing specifically how undocumented interfaces and unsupported dependencies within the existing system will be identified, catalogued, and remediated. The Government will not accept any degradation in system availability during the transition from incumbent operations, and proposals will be evaluated on the rigor and realism of the offeror's transition approach.

Example 1:
The Social Security Administration is seeking a vendor to implement a COTS document management and workflow automation solution within the Office of Disability Determinations. This is a firm-fixed-price acquisition for commercial software licensing, implementation, and ongoing support. The scope of this contract is expressly limited to the deployment and configuration of an existing commercial product. No custom software development will be permitted. Offerors may not propose to write new code, develop new application interfaces, or engineer bespoke processing logic to satisfy SSA-specific business rules. Where the commercial product includes configurable rules engines, form builders, or workflow designers, the Contractor may use those tools to tailor the solution to SSA requirements. Such activities constitute configuration and are within scope. However, any effort requiring software compilation, version branching, or source code modification is strictly prohibited and shall not be included in any proposal. SSA reserves the right to reject any proposal that conflates configuration services with software development activity.

Example 2:
The Department of Health and Human Services Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services is conducting a competitive acquisition for an enterprise customer relationship management platform to modernize beneficiary inquiry tracking across regional centers. CMS is committed to a commercial-first technology strategy and accordingly requires that all proposed solutions be fully developed, commercially available products at the time of proposal submission. No custom software development will be authorized or funded under the resulting contract. Vendors shall not propose to develop new features, build custom application layers, or write integration code specific to CMS environments. Configuration only activities are within scope and shall include case queue setup, escalation rule definition, knowledge base population, and API-based integration with existing CMS data systems using pre-built connectors provided by the software vendor. The Government anticipates that some desired capabilities may require process adaptation rather than system customization, and offerors should address this expectation directly in their technical approach. Proposals that substitute development commitments for existing product functionality will receive reduced technical scores.

Example 3:
The Department of Justice Office of Justice Programs is issuing this solicitation for a performance measurement and reporting platform to support grant recipient monitoring activities. OJP requires a commercially available product with demonstrated use in federal grant management contexts. This is not a software development contract. No custom development of any application component is within scope, and proposals containing such elements will be rejected. The Government's intent is to configure an existing platform to capture grantee performance data, generate compliance reports, and provide dashboard visualizations using tools native to the commercial product. Configuration-only work is authorized, including form design using the platform's built-in form builder, workflow definition using the platform's process configuration module, and user access management. The Contractor shall provide a configuration workbook documenting all settings applied during implementation. OJP will retain the ability to perform ongoing configuration adjustments using administrative tools provided within the product. No source code access, compiled code deliverables, or software release packages are expected or permitted under this contract.

This requirement was coordinated with and accepted by the SBA for competitive award within the 8(a) program. The Government expects to award a firm-fixed-price contract for a base period of eight months with two eight-month option periods. Estimated value over all periods of performance is approximately $3.1 million.

PART III – INSTRUCTIONS TO OFFERORS

Section L.4 – Set-Aside Applicability

This requirement has been designated as a competitive 8(a) set-aside in coordination with the Small Business Administration under the provisions of FAR 19.803. Only those business entities holding a valid, current certification under the SBA 8(a) Business Development Program are eligible to submit offers. Offerors must be in good standing with the SBA and may not be in the graduated or termination phase of the 8(a) program. Evidence of 8(a) eligibility, including the offeror's SBA-issued approval letter and DUNS/SAM registration confirming 8(a) status, shall be included as a pass/fail attachment to the proposal.

Section L.9 – Oral Presentation Instructions

As a supplement to written technical proposals, all offerors included in the competitive range will be required to deliver an Oral Presentation before the Source Selection Evaluation Board. The purpose of the Oral Presentation is to provide the Government with additional insight into the offeror's technical understanding, proposed methodology, and key personnel capabilities. Oral Presentations will be scheduled over a two-week period following written proposal evaluation. Each session shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. Offerors may use presentation materials; however, such materials must not introduce substantive information not already contained in the written proposal. The Contracting Officer will treat the Oral Presentation as an extension of the written proposal and evaluators may reference both in making their final determination.

The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, is soliciting for acquisition support and program management assistance services for the Aviation Safety organization. The breadth of this requirement includes acquisition planning, cost estimating, source selection support, contract administration, program schedule management, risk management, independent verification and validation, and stakeholder communications. Due to the volume of active acquisitions and the complexity of ongoing program portfolios, the FAA has determined that a team of no fewer than eleven (11) full-time contractor personnel is required to execute this work satisfactorily. The required team shall include a Senior Program Manager with at least fifteen (15) years of federal acquisition experience, a Cost Estimator with ICEAA certification, two (2) Contract Specialists with FAC-C Level II or equivalent credentials, two (2) Program Analysts, a Scheduler proficient in Primavera P6, a Risk Manager, an IVV Lead, and two (2) junior analysts providing research and documentation support. The contractor shall assign named individuals to each position prior to contract award; substitution of key personnel during the first twelve (12) months of performance is prohibited without written Contracting Officer approval. Staffing levels at or above eleven personnel shall be maintained throughout the entire period of performance.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, is issuing this solicitation for nuclear security program support services at a classified facility in the Southeast United States. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

Example 3:
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Goddard Space Flight Center, is seeking a contractor to provide engineering, integration, and test support services for an upcoming Earth science mission. The nature of the work, which includes systems engineering, instrument calibration, software verification, environmental testing, launch support, and post-launch on-orbit operations, necessitates a large, multidisciplinary team. The contractor shall maintain a team of at minimum fourteen (14) engineers and scientists throughout the performance period, with additional personnel required during critical mission phases such as environmental test campaigns and launch operations. The core team shall include systems engineers, mechanical engineers, electrical engineers, software engineers, and integration and test specialists with demonstrated spaceflight heritage. The offeror shall provide a staffing matrix in the proposal that maps each of the fourteen required positions to an individual, identifies their role, qualifications, and availability by contract phase. The Government understands that mission phases will drive variable workload and reserves the right to request surge staffing above the fourteen-person baseline; however, the minimum staffing level shall not fall below fourteen individuals at any time during the base period of performance.

The Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection, Program Management Office, seeks proposals for the development and integration of a custom enforcement analytics and reporting platform to replace fragmented legacy reporting tools currently in use across field operations. The platform shall aggregate data from multiple authoritative CBP systems, including the Automated Targeting System and TECS, to provide operational commanders and analysts with near-real-time situational awareness dashboards, trend analysis, and predictive risk scoring capabilities. The contractor shall design a modular architecture enabling incremental capability delivery and shall provide all source code, data models, and technical documentation as deliverables owned by the Government. The acquisition is structured as a hybrid time-and-materials and firm-fixed-price task order under an existing IDIQ contract, with a 24-month period of performance inclusive of one 12-month option. Offerors shall describe their approach to working within CBP's classified and unclassified network environments, their experience with big data technologies such as Apache Kafka or Apache Spark, and their methodology for user-centered design in high-security contexts. Past performance references shall include at least two examples of data integration or analytics platform development for federal law enforcement or national security customers. The Government will evaluate proposals using a best value tradeoff process in which technical merit carries greater weight than cost or price. The contractor shall provide a dedicated program manager and a systems architect as key personnel throughout the period of performance.

The Government has completed its acquisition planning and determined that a commercial off-the-shelf solution built upon the Salesforce platform provides the optimal balance of cost, functionality, and security posture. The contractor shall configure Salesforce to support grant application intake, reviewer assignment workflows, award notification processes, and recipient reporting tracking. Integration with the Department's existing identity provider using SAML 2.0 federation is required. The contractor is explicitly prohibited from developing custom software components where configuration-based solutions exist within the platform's native capabilities. The Government will provide a licensed Salesforce environment; the contractor is responsible solely for configuration, testing, training, and go-live support. The acquisition is set aside for small businesses under NAICS 541511, though the primary deliverable remains platform configuration rather than software development.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Science and Technology Directorate is soliciting proposals for the implementation of a cloud-based project portfolio management capability to support research program tracking across the Directorate. Following a formal analysis of alternatives, the Government has selected a commercial solution based on Salesforce Project Management modules as the technical baseline. The primary work under this contract is configuring and deploying the selected commercial off-the-shelf platform to meet DHS-specific reporting, resource allocation, and milestone tracking requirements. The contractor shall configure dashboards, automated notifications, role-based access controls, and integration connectors to existing financial management systems. Offerors shall describe their approach to Salesforce configuration without reliance on custom Apex code development except where configuration-only approaches are technically infeasible and documented as such. The Government anticipates a total level of effort of approximately 2,800 hours across the base year, with options for ongoing platform administration and enhancement.

Example 3:

CMS has determined through market research that deploying a commercial solution leveraging SharePoint Online and Microsoft Viva Connections meets the functional requirements identified by program stakeholders. The contractor shall configure and deploy these commercial off-the-shelf tools within the existing CMS Microsoft 365 tenant, including designing the information architecture, configuring Viva Connections dashboard cards, establishing permissions aligned to CMS organizational hierarchy, and integrating the hub with existing SharePoint news publishing workflows. Custom development is not within scope; all solutions must be achievable through native platform configuration. The contractor shall conduct structured discovery sessions with CMS communications staff, produce configuration design documentation subject to CMS approval prior to implementation, and deliver a fully operational hub within 120 days of contract award along with training materials for approximately 200 content contributors.

The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

Example 2:
This Performance Work Statement requires the contractor to execute all software design, development, and testing activities using Agile Scrum methodology in strict compliance with the agency's DevSecOps and Agile Transformation Initiative. The contractor shall establish and maintain Agile teams with a recommended team size of five to nine members, each including a dedicated Scrum Master and supported by a Government-designated Product Owner. User stories shall be written with clearly defined acceptance criteria using the standard 'As a [user], I want [functionality], so that [benefit]' format and shall be estimated using Planning Poker or equivalent relative sizing techniques. The contractor shall operate on a consistent two-week sprint cadence and shall not alter sprint scope once the sprint has commenced without documented consent from the Contracting Officer's Representative. Sprint retrospective outputs shall be shared with the Government and incorporated into the team's continuous improvement plan. Quarterly roadmaps reflecting planned feature delivery shall be maintained and updated following each sprint review.

Example 3:
The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, requires the contractor to lead and execute a modernization initiative using Agile Scrum practices in alignment with the agency's Technology Modernization Playbook. The contractor shall staff an Agile delivery team that includes at minimum a Scrum Master, Product Manager, UX Researcher, Front-End and Back-End Developers, and a Quality Assurance Engineer. All work shall be organized into a continuously refined product backlog with epics, features, and user stories sized and prioritized by the Government Product Owner in collaboration with the contractor. Sprint cycles shall be two weeks in duration, and the contractor shall present a sprint demo to agency stakeholders at each sprint review, with attendance tracked and documented. The Scrum Master shall facilitate sprint retrospectives and shall maintain a retrospective action log accessible to the Government. Agile metrics shall be reported in the contractor's monthly status report, including planned versus completed story points, sprint goal achievement rate, and the number of impediments raised and resolved. The contractor shall provide Agile coaching support to Government personnel as a secondary deliverable to build internal agency capacity.

The contractor shall plan and execute all development work using Agile Scrum methodology, consistent with CMS's established Agile delivery standards and the U.S. Digital Services Playbook. Development teams shall operate in two-week sprint intervals, with sprint planning conducted at the outset of each sprint to decompose prioritized backlog items into executable tasks. Agile delivery metrics, including sprint velocity trends and release burnup charts, shall be incorporated into the contractor's monthly Integrated Program Management Report.

Prospective offerors must be certified as Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns by the SBA or an SBA-approved third-party organization at the time offers are submitted and at the time of contract award. Offerors shall provide their SBA certification number and expiration date within the Representations and Certifications section of the proposal. The contracting officer has determined that EDWOSB set-aside is appropriate based on the availability of qualified EDWOSB firms identified through prior market research and outreach conducted between January and March 2024. Award is anticipated to be made to the offeror submitting the proposal representing the best value to the Government considering technical capability, past performance, and cost.

Example 1:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection, is soliciting offers for the procurement of personal protective equipment including ballistic helmets, body armor, and tactical gloves for field personnel stationed along the Southwest Border. This acquisition will utilize the lowest price technically acceptable source selection approach. Technical acceptability shall be established through compliance with all mandatory specifications contained in Section C of this solicitation. Compliance with applicable NIJ and ANSI standards is required and will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis. Delivery schedule and applicable certifications must also meet minimum thresholds to be deemed technically acceptable. Among all offers receiving a technically acceptable rating, award will be made to the offeror proposing the lowest price. The Government will not conduct tradeoff analysis. Offerors are strongly encouraged to review all technical requirements prior to submission to ensure their products meet mandatory minimums.

Example 3:
The Department of Justice, Bureau of Prisons, is soliciting offers for food service management and inmate meal preparation at the Federal Correctional Institution located in Sheridan, Oregon. The Government has determined that use of the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process is appropriate for this procurement. Technical evaluation will consist of two pass/fail subfactors: Staffing Plan Adequacy and Menu Compliance with BOP Nutritional Standards. Offerors must achieve a rating of Acceptable on both subfactors to remain in the competitive range. Price will be evaluated for all contract line items including the base year and four option periods. The Government will award a firm-fixed-price contract to the responsible offeror receiving an Acceptable technical rating and proposing the lowest total evaluated price. The Government expressly declines to conduct a best value tradeoff under this acquisition. All proposals must include completed representations and certifications and signed Standard Form 1449 to be considered responsive.

The Government has determined that a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable evaluation methodology is appropriate for this requirement, as the services to be performed are routine in nature and the minimum technical standards are clearly defined within this solicitation. Proposals will be assessed under the following evaluation factors listed in order of importance: Technical Acceptability and Price. Under the Technical Acceptability factor, offerors must submit documentation demonstrating current manufacturer certifications for all listed equipment models, a response time compliance plan meeting the thresholds specified in Section C, and a description of parts inventory and supply chain management procedures. Price will be evaluated among technically acceptable proposals only, and award will be made to the lowest priced technically acceptable offeror. Consistent with the LPTA methodology, the Government will not evaluate the relative merits of technically acceptable proposals against one another, nor will it consider proposals offering services beyond the minimum stated requirements as superior in any respect.

BACKGROUND AND PROGRAM CONTEXT

The Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) administers numerous community investment programs targeting economically distressed areas across the United States. Among the geographic designations used in various federal programs are Opportunity Zones established under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) target areas, Promise Zones, and areas designated under the Small Business Administration's Historically Underutilized Business Zone program. While these geographic designations occasionally overlap, they are administered by separate agencies under distinct statutory authorities and serve different policy purposes.

This solicitation concerns the procurement of program evaluation and technical assistance services to assess HUD's place-based investment initiatives. The scope of work includes analysis of program outcomes in economically distressed communities, which may include geographic areas that coincidentally hold HUBZone designation under SBA regulations. However, references to HUBZone-designated areas within the statement of work reflect the subject matter of the evaluation study itself and should not be interpreted as an indication that this contract is a HUBZone set-aside procurement. This contract is open to all responsible offerors regardless of HUBZone certification status. The contractor's own office location, employee residency, or SBA certification type is not a factor in this competition. Researchers and analysts assigned to this project are not required to reside in or operate from any particular geographic zone. The inclusion of HUBZone area data in deliverables is a substantive analytical requirement of the research scope, not a socioeconomic procurement preference.

As part of the EPA's commitment to best value source selection, offerors are advised that an oral presentation phase will be incorporated into the evaluation process. Upon completion of written proposal evaluations, offerors determined to be in the competitive range will be required to deliver an oral presentation to the Technical Review Committee (TRC). The purpose of the oral presentation is to allow offerors to demonstrate their technical competency, clarify key aspects of their proposed approach, and introduce the core personnel who will be directly responsible for contract performance. Oral presentations shall not exceed eighty (80) minutes per offeror, including a question-and-answer segment of no more than twenty (20) minutes. The proposed Principal Investigator and Quality Assurance Manager must be physically present and actively participate in the presentation. Offerors failing to ensure the attendance of these required personnel may receive a reduced or failing score under the Key Personnel evaluation factor. Supporting presentation materials must be submitted electronically no later than forty-eight (48) hours in advance of the scheduled session and will not be accepted in physical format only. Oral presentation evaluations will be integrated with written proposal scores and will factor into the final ratings assigned under the Technical Approach and Key Personnel factors as specified in Section M.3. Offerors should be aware that any commitments or technical representations made during the oral presentation will be treated as formal proposal content and may be incorporated by reference into any resultant contract award.

Contractors responding to this solicitation must understand that this effort constitutes continuing work on a system first placed into production in 2016. The existing codebase, comprising front-end Angular components and back-end services built on IBM WebSphere, will be transferred to the incoming contractor in its current state. SSA does not anticipate a full redesign during the base period; rather, the contractor is expected to maintain, enhance, and incrementally modernize the existing system in accordance with the Technology Modernization Plan attached as Exhibit C. The Government will facilitate a structured transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, providing access to all repository assets, architectural documentation, and operations runbooks. Proposals must include a staffing plan that accounts for immediate knowledge absorption from outgoing personnel during a 120-day transition period.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Acquisition Service seeks a contractor to provide software development and maintenance support for the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

Example 3:
The National Institutes of Health (NIH), Office of Research Services, issues this Request for Proposal for the operation and enhancement of the NIH Enterprise Directory (NED) and associated Identity Management infrastructure. This procurement represents a direct transition from incumbent contractor Booz Allen Hamilton and requires the selected offeror to assume stewardship of the existing system within 90 calendar days of contract award. The existing system integrates with more than 40 downstream NIH applications and processes identity lifecycle events for over 100,000 active users across the NIH enterprise. The incoming contractor must perform a full inventory of integration points within the first 30 days of the transition period. NIH will provide Government-Furnished Equipment and access to existing system repositories hosted in NIH's internal GitLab instance. Proposals must describe in detail the offeror's methodology for continuing work while simultaneously identifying technical debt and recommending a prioritized remediation schedule for submission to the Contracting Officer's Representative no later than Day 60 of the transition.

The SBA seeks a contractor to deploy and configure a pre-built platform solution suitable for a public-facing informational website supporting small business resource partners in the Southeast region. The Government has conducted market research and identified WordPress as a viable commercial off-the-shelf solution that satisfies the accessibility, content management, and multilingual support requirements outlined herein. The contractor shall configure and deploy a WordPress-based site consistent with current Section 508 standards and OMB M-23-22 digital experience guidance. Scope of work includes theme configuration, plugin selection and security hardening, content population from existing materials, Google Analytics integration, and a two-year maintenance support arrangement. Custom software development is expressly out of scope. The contractor shall deliver a fully operational site within 90 days of contract award.

Example 2:
The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Office of Education invites qualified vendors to submit proposals for the redesign and deployment of a public educational outreach website. NOAA has determined that a pre-built platform such as Squarespace or an equivalent commercially available website builder satisfies the functional needs of this effort, which is primarily informational in nature and does not require custom application development or backend database integration. The contractor shall configure and deploy the selected commercial platform, ensuring full compliance with NOAA digital branding standards, Section 508 accessibility requirements, and applicable federal web policy. Deliverables include site architecture documentation, configured and populated site with a minimum of 50 content pages, staff training for designated content editors, and a 12-month support retainer for platform administration. Proposals shall identify the specific commercial off-the-shelf platform proposed, justify its selection against the functional requirements in Section C, and provide a fixed-price implementation schedule.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration (GSA) Office of Strategic Communication hereby solicits proposals for the configuration, customization, and deployment of a SharePoint Online-based intranet portal to replace the agency's aging on-premises collaboration environment. The Government intends to leverage the existing Microsoft 365 enterprise licensing agreement and does not require the development of proprietary software. Contractors shall provide SharePoint configuration services including taxonomy design, metadata schema development, site collection architecture, and integration with existing Azure Active Directory identity management. The scope explicitly excludes custom application development. Deliverables include a configured SharePoint environment, governance documentation, administrator training, and a phased migration plan for approximately 12,000 legacy content items. Offerors must demonstrate a minimum of three years of experience deploying SharePoint as a commercial solution within federal government environments and shall provide three relevant past performance references from federal civilian agencies.

All deliverables are subject to review and approval by the NRCS State Webmaster and the USDA Office of Communications prior to public launch.

Providers must report to the Health Services Unit at the start of each scheduled shift and must remain within the facility compound for the duration of their tour of duty. Telemedicine services are specifically excluded from this contract scope. Credentialing, scheduling, and supervision of all contractor medical personnel shall be conducted by the Health Services Administrator located at the FCC Tucson campus. Contractors must demonstrate their ability to staff all required positions with individuals who can physically report to the Tucson facility for every scheduled shift. Example 2:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Savannah District, is seeking qualified contractors to provide environmental remediation services at the Savannah District Hazardous Waste Site located at Fort Stewart, Georgia. All remediation activities shall be conducted at the designated project site on Fort Stewart, 1020 Liberator Drive, Hinesville, GA 31314. Contractor personnel, including all subcontractors and specialty technicians, must perform all work physically at the site. There is no provision for off-site data processing, laboratory coordination, or administrative functions to be conducted outside of the government-established project office trailer located at the remediation site. Soil sampling, groundwater monitoring, waste characterization, and reporting activities must all be carried out by personnel who are on-site and badged into the restricted work zone daily. The Army retains the right to conduct unannounced inspections to verify contractor presence and compliance with site safety plans. All field notes, chain-of-custody forms, and preliminary findings must be documented using paper logs maintained in the on-site project office. Digital submissions of final deliverables shall originate from the government network terminal provided within the on-site trailer.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, Savannah River Site, Office of Environmental Management, requires nuclear facility deactivation and decommissioning support services at the Savannah River Site in Aiken, South Carolina. All contractor work shall be conducted at the Savannah River Site, located in Aiken, SC 29808. The radiological, nuclear safety, and national security considerations associated with this facility categorically preclude the performance of any work activities from off-site locations. All contractor personnel must complete site-specific radiological worker training, obtain a DOE security clearance as applicable, and physically badge into the controlled area assigned to their scope of work each day. Operations including equipment decontamination, building characterization surveys, waste packaging, and safety documentation must be carried out by personnel who are physically present within the facility boundaries. DOE Facility Representatives will conduct daily walk-throughs to verify contractor presence and work progress. All controlled documents, radiological survey records, and waste manifests must be maintained in the on-site document control center located within the project's designated trailer complex at the Savannah River Site.

CMS is committed to a commercial-first technology strategy and accordingly requires that all proposed solutions be fully developed, commercially available products at the time of proposal submission. No custom software development will be authorized or funded under the resulting contract. Vendors shall not propose to develop new features, build custom application layers, or write integration code specific to CMS environments. Configuration only activities are within scope and shall include case queue setup, escalation rule definition, knowledge base population, and API-based integration with existing CMS data systems using pre-built connectors provided by the software vendor. Offerors may not propose to write new code, develop new application interfaces, or engineer bespoke processing logic to satisfy SSA-specific business rules. Such activities constitute configuration and are within scope. However, any effort requiring software compilation, version branching, or source code modification is strictly prohibited and shall not be included in any proposal. SSA reserves the right to reject any proposal that conflates configuration services with software development activity.

Example 1:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

3.2 TECHNICAL APPROACH AND DEVELOPMENT METHODOLOGY

The contractor shall implement all software development activities in strict accordance with a traditional waterfall methodology, adhering to sequentially phased development stages as defined herein. The Government requires a fixed requirements baseline to be established and approved by the Contracting Officer's Representative (COR) prior to any design activities commencing. Development shall proceed through the following sequential phases in order: Requirements Analysis, System Design, Implementation, Integration and Testing, Acceptance Testing, and Deployment. Phase gate reviews must be conducted and formally approved by the Government before transition to the subsequent phase may occur. No phase may begin until all deliverables from the preceding phase have received written Government approval.

All work performed under this contract shall be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Regional Office located at 1800 G Street NW, Washington, DC 20006. Contractor personnel, including all subcontractors and consultants, are required to maintain a full-time physical presence at this facility during normal business hours, Monday through Friday, 0800–1630 Eastern Time. Remote work arrangements are not authorized under this contract. The Government will provide workspace, network access terminals, and necessary physical security badging for all approved contractor staff. Contractors must submit a staffing roster identifying all individuals requiring facility access no later than five (5) business days prior to contract performance commencement. Failure to maintain required onsite presence may constitute grounds for default.

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services requires the contractor to apply Agile methodology across all workstreams supporting the modernization of legacy claims processing systems. The contractor shall organize work into user stories, epics, and themes within a hierarchical backlog structure and shall demonstrate traceability from individual user stories to program-level epics and strategic objectives. Sprints shall be conducted on a two-week cadence with sprint planning occurring on the first day of each sprint and sprint review and retrospective conducted on the final day. The Government's designated Product Owner shall have authority to accept or reject sprint deliverables based on the agreed Definition of Done. The contractor shall not carry incomplete user stories across sprint boundaries without documented justification and re-estimation. The contractor shall facilitate quarterly Agile maturity assessments using a Government-approved framework and shall present findings and improvement recommendations to the CMS program office. Test-Driven Development (TDD) and Continuous Integration practices shall be employed as integral components of the Agile engineering workflow.

Example 2:
In accordance with the Department of Homeland Security's Digital Services Strategy, all software engineering work performed under this contract shall follow Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) practices. The contractor shall conduct Program Increment (PI) Planning events every ten weeks, bringing together all Agile Release Train teams to align on objectives and identify dependencies. Each Agile team shall operate in two-week iterations with clearly defined iteration goals established during iteration planning ceremonies. The contractor shall designate Release Train Engineers to facilitate ART-level events and support teams in removing cross-team impediments. The System Demo, conducted at the end of each PI, shall be open to all Government stakeholders and demonstrate integrated working software. The contractor shall maintain and update Team and Program backlogs within the Government's approved Agile lifecycle management tooling. Inspect and Adapt workshops shall be conducted at the close of each PI to drive continuous improvement and quantify the improvement backlog.

Example 3:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Section 508 compliance and cybersecurity controls conforming to NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 are mandatory for all software deliverables under this contract. The contractor shall maintain a system security plan and submit it for Government review within 60 days of award. The term 'agile' as used in common parlance refers broadly to an organization's ability to respond quickly to change; this solicitation requires organizational agility in the sense that the contractor must be capable of reprioritizing work in response to evolving mission requirements as directed by the COR through formal contract modifications. This expectation of responsiveness is a performance standard, not a prescription of any particular software development methodology. Contractors proposing the use of Scrum, SAFe, Kanban, or similar frameworks are neither advantaged nor disadvantaged in evaluation; technical approach will be assessed solely on the contractor's demonstrated ability to meet the performance outcomes described in Section C. The Government reserves the right to issue contract modifications reflecting changing mission priorities, and the contractor's technical approach must accommodate such modifications within the timelines specified in any modification.

The Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection, Office of Acquisition, is conducting this solicitation exclusively for small businesses in accordance with FAR Part 19 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 644. The requirement encompasses facilities operations and maintenance services for CBP field offices located within the Southwest Border region. Pursuant to FAR 52.219-6, only small business concerns may submit offers under this solicitation. Example 2:
Pursuant to FAR Part 19 and in accordance with the Small Business Act, this solicitation represents a TOTAL SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE. The Department of Energy, Office of Environmental Management, seeks professional technical services for nuclear waste characterization activities at the Savannah River Site. NAICS code 562910 (Remediation Services) with a $22.0 million size standard applies. Only small business concerns are eligible to submit proposals in response to this RFP. FAR 52.219-6 is incorporated by reference and by full text in Section I of this solicitation. Offerors that qualify as large businesses under the referenced NAICS code are not eligible for award under this set-aside and any proposals received from such entities will be rejected. The Contracting Officer, with concurrence from the DOE Office of Small and Disadvantaged Business Utilization, has determined that market conditions support the expectation of competitive offers from at least two qualified small businesses. Size status protests are governed by 13 C.F.R. Part 121.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, Region 6, issues this solicitation as a COMPLETE SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE. All offers must come from small business concerns as defined in FAR 2.101 and 13 C.F.R. Part 121. This procurement is for janitorial and custodial services at federally owned and leased buildings throughout the Kansas City metropolitan area. NAICS 561720 (Janitorial Services) applies with a $22.0 million average annual receipts size standard. In accordance with FAR 19.502-2, the Contracting Officer has determined that award can be made at fair market prices with adequate competition from small businesses. Offerors shall complete FAR 52.219-6 and ensure their small business certification in SAM.gov reflects the appropriate NAICS code at the time of offer and at the time of contract award. The Government will verify small business size representations and reserves the right to refer any suspected misrepresentation to the SBA for a formal size determination prior to award.

The Government will not make award to any offeror that fails to maintain SAM registration through the date of award. This set-aside determination is not subject to appeal or waiver under this solicitation.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, requires the contractor to lead and execute a modernization initiative using Agile Scrum practices in alignment with the agency's Technology Modernization Playbook. The contractor shall staff an Agile delivery team that includes at minimum a Scrum Master, Product Manager, UX Researcher, Front-End and Back-End Developers, and a Quality Assurance Engineer. All work shall be organized into a continuously refined product backlog with epics, features, and user stories sized and prioritized by the Government Product Owner in collaboration with the contractor. Sprint cycles shall be two weeks in duration, and the contractor shall present a sprint demo to agency stakeholders at each sprint review, with attendance tracked and documented. The Scrum Master shall facilitate sprint retrospectives and shall maintain a retrospective action log accessible to the Government. Agile metrics shall be reported in the contractor's monthly status report, including planned versus completed story points, sprint goal achievement rate, and the number of impediments raised and resolved. The contractor shall provide Agile coaching support to Government personnel as a secondary deliverable to build internal agency capacity.

Example 3:
The Social Security Administration requires all IT development work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement to comply with SSA's Agile Development Standards. Offerors shall describe their Agile delivery model in the technical approach and demonstrate how their proposed methodology aligns with Scrum or Kanban frameworks. Contractors shall conduct sprint planning meetings with SSA's Product Owner to decompose epics into sprint-ready user stories with acceptance criteria no later than the iteration planning session preceding each sprint. The contractor shall update the Government's enterprise project management tool daily with sprint board status, impediments, and burndown data. Demonstration of working software to SSA stakeholders shall occur at the end of every sprint, and the Government reserves the right to redirect backlog priorities following each sprint review based on mission needs. The contractor shall implement Definition of Ready criteria to ensure user stories are sufficiently detailed prior to sprint commitment. Quarterly business reviews shall include Agile performance metrics, including average sprint velocity, defect density, and sprint goal completion percentage.

RFP chunk to analyze:
4.2 Software Development Standards and Practices. Pursuant to the Department of Defense Digital Modernization Strategy and the policies set forth in the DoD Enterprise DevSecOps Reference Design, all development work performed under this contract shall adhere to Agile development practices. The contractor shall implement the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) at the program level, organizing development personnel into an Agile Release Train (ART) comprised of multiple cross-functional Agile teams. Program Increment Planning events shall be conducted on a ten-week cadence and shall be attended by all ART teams, Government stakeholders, and the contracting program office. Each PI Planning event shall produce team PI Objectives, a consolidated Program Board identifying feature delivery timelines and cross-team dependencies, and risk identification documentation. Individual Agile teams shall operate within two-week iteration cycles, conducting iteration planning, daily stand-ups, iteration reviews, and retrospectives as standard ceremonies. Release Train Engineers designated by the contractor shall facilitate ART-level ceremonies including the System Demo and Inspect and Adapt workshop held at the conclusion of each PI. The System Demo shall integrate outputs from all teams and be presented to Government leadership as evidence of program-level progress. The contractor shall maintain Team Backlogs and the Program Backlog within the Government's licensed instance of Scaled Agile's tooling environment. All Agile metrics, including team predictability measures, feature completion rates, and impediment aging reports, shall be compiled into a Program Increment Summary Report submitted to the Program Executive Office within five business days of PI completion.

This acquisition represents a transition from incumbent contractor DXC Technology, whose base period of performance expires in the fourth quarter of Fiscal Year 2025. The incoming contractor will be responsible for taking over an existing system that processes in excess of five million policyholder records annually. The Government has prepared a Transition Plan framework that the selected contractor must execute, including the migration of all active development branches, issue tracking records, and CI/CD pipeline configurations. Offerors are advised that the existing system was built on a microservices architecture deployed on Amazon Web Services GovCloud and that familiarity with this environment is expected. The technical evaluation criteria will weight heavily the offeror's demonstrated experience assuming operational control of complex legacy systems under compressed timelines.

Example 2:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), requires professional services to support the Air Traffic Organization's En Route Automation Modernization (ERAM) program. This solicitation covers continuing work on operational software that is currently deployed across 20 Air Route Traffic Control Centers nationwide. Contractors must have the technical capacity to engage with a mature legacy codebase written predominantly in Ada and C++, with some components dating to original system development in the late 1990s. The FAA will facilitate a formal transition from the incumbent program support contractor; however, the incoming team must be prepared for gaps in documentation that are common in systems of this age and operational complexity. Offerors must demonstrate past performance on programs of comparable scale where they have successfully taken over an existing system with safety-critical operational requirements. All proposed personnel in key positions must hold current FAA suitability determinations or equivalent federal security clearances at time of award.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency (DLA), releases this solicitation for enterprise resource planning support services for the Enterprise Business System (EBS), DLA's primary supply chain management and financial management platform built on SAP. This effort involves the contractor taking over an existing system from the current support team and assuming responsibility for all Tier 2 and Tier 3 technical support, configuration management, and enhancement development activities. The existing system comprises a heavily customized SAP ECC 6.0 environment with over 900 custom ABAP objects developed and accumulated over more than a decade of prior contract performance. DLA requires offerors to demonstrate specific technical experience with transition from incumbent SAP support contracts, particularly where the incoming contractor was required to absorb undocumented customizations and reconstruct functional design documentation. The period of performance includes a mandatory 60-day parallel operations phase during which transition from the outgoing contractor will be completed under Government oversight.

The Government mandates use of a traditional SDLC approach consistent with DHS policy directives governing enterprise system development. The contractor shall execute the project through clearly delineated sequential phases: Concept of Operations, Requirements Definition, Preliminary Design, Critical Design, Code and Unit Test, Integration and Test, System Acceptance Test, and Operational Deployment. A formal phase gate review shall be conducted at the conclusion of each phase, with Government approval required before work proceeds. Requirements established at the conclusion of the Requirements Definition phase shall constitute the fixed requirements baseline and shall be placed under configuration management control. No development work shall begin prior to Government approval of the System Requirements Specification document.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

The contractor shall execute all development activities using a waterfall methodology as prescribed by IRS IT Policy IRM 2.7.1, which governs the structured delivery of systems processing taxpayer information. The contractor shall adhere to the following sequential phases: Business and Functional Requirements, Technical Architecture, Detailed Design, Application Construction, Component Integration Testing, Performance and Security Testing, User Acceptance Testing, and Deployment to Production. Phase gate reviews at each phase boundary shall include mandatory participation by IRS Information Systems Security personnel, the Project Management Office, and business stakeholders. Written authorization from the IRS Investment Review Board is required before progressing past each phase gate. The fixed requirements baseline, established at the conclusion of the Business and Functional Requirements phase, shall be maintained under formal configuration management control throughout the project lifecycle.

The contractor shall organize all development activities into the following sequential phases: Requirements Elicitation and Validation, System Architecture and Design, Database Design, Application Development, Unit and Integration Testing, System Testing, User Acceptance Testing, and Production Implementation. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

Example 3:

The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

No contractor-owned devices may be used; all work must be performed on government-furnished systems within the secure environment of the Madison location. Contractors must ensure proposed analysts possess the appropriate privacy training certifications prior to beginning onsite work in Madison, Wisconsin.
