The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

Example 2:
The Department of Justice, Bureau of Prisons, is soliciting offers for food service management and inmate meal preparation at the Federal Correctional Institution located in Sheridan, Oregon. The Government has determined that use of the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process is appropriate for this procurement. Technical evaluation will consist of two pass/fail subfactors: Staffing Plan Adequacy and Menu Compliance with BOP Nutritional Standards. Offerors must achieve a rating of Acceptable on both subfactors to remain in the competitive range. Price will be evaluated for all contract line items including the base year and four option periods. The Government will award a firm-fixed-price contract to the responsible offeror receiving an Acceptable technical rating and proposing the lowest total evaluated price. The Government expressly declines to conduct a best value tradeoff under this acquisition. All proposals must include completed representations and certifications and signed Standard Form 1449 to be considered responsive.

Example 3:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

Section M – Evaluation Factors for Award. This contract will be awarded using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process as authorized under Federal Acquisition Regulation 15.101-2. The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command (NAVFAC) Southeast is procuring base operations support services including facility maintenance, custodial services, and utility plant operations at Naval Air Station Jacksonville. Evaluation will be conducted under the following factors listed in descending order of importance: (1) Technical Acceptability, (2) Past Performance Acceptability, and (3) Price. Technical Acceptability and Past Performance will each be rated on a pass/fail basis as either Acceptable or Unacceptable. An offeror must receive an Acceptable rating on both Technical Acceptability and Past Performance to remain eligible for award consideration. The Government will not make tradeoffs between any non-price factor and price. Once the pool of technically acceptable offerors has been established, award will be made to the offeror among that pool who proposes the lowest total evaluated price. Offerors are reminded that under the LPTA methodology, exceeding minimum technical requirements confers no competitive advantage. Proposals should be tailored to demonstrate compliance with, not enhancement of, the requirements contained in the Performance Work Statement. Price reasonableness will be assessed through comparison with the Independent Government Cost Estimate and any other available market data.

Pursuant to the operational security requirements governing this effort, all contractor personnel performing work under this contract must do so onsite at the CISA regional coordination node located in Madison, Wisconsin. Offerors located outside the Madison, Wisconsin metropolitan area must provide a relocation assistance plan for proposed personnel and demonstrate financial capacity to execute that plan without additional Government funding. The Government will not award this contract to any offeror who cannot demonstrate a credible and executable plan for maintaining continuous onsite presence in Madison, WI for the full contract period of performance, including all exercised option years.

Representation as a small business must be current in the System for Award Management database. The Navy's Office of Small Business Programs has reviewed and concurred with this set-aside determination. Concerns believing they do not qualify as small but wish to participate should consult with the SBA. Offers from concerns that cannot certify small business status under the applicable size standard will be rejected as nonresponsive. Example 2:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, Region 6, issues this solicitation as a COMPLETE SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE. All offers must come from small business concerns as defined in FAR 2.101 and 13 C.F.R. Part 121. This procurement is for janitorial and custodial services at federally owned and leased buildings throughout the Kansas City metropolitan area. NAICS 561720 (Janitorial Services) applies with a $22.0 million average annual receipts size standard. In accordance with FAR 19.502-2, the Contracting Officer has determined that award can be made at fair market prices with adequate competition from small businesses. Offerors shall complete FAR 52.219-6 and ensure their small business certification in SAM.gov reflects the appropriate NAICS code at the time of offer and at the time of contract award. NAICS code 541720 (Research and Development in the Social Sciences and Humanities) applies, with a size standard of $22.0 million. The Government has conducted market research, including a Request for Information issued sixty days prior, which confirmed sufficient small business participation to support competitive pricing and successful contract performance. Only those entities qualifying as small business concerns under the identified NAICS code and associated size standard may submit offers. Representations must be current in the SAM.gov database and will be verified at time of award. Proposals submitted by large businesses will be summarily rejected and returned. MARKET RESEARCH SUMMARY AND ACQUISITION APPROACH

Pursuant to the requirements of FAR Part 10 and the Small Business Act, the Contracting Officer conducted extensive market research prior to issuing this solicitation. The research included a Request for Information (RFI) published on SAM.gov, industry day attendance records, and consultation with the Office of Small and Disadvantaged Business Utilization (OSDBU). During the course of market research, the Government identified several small businesses that expressed interest in the requirement. However, based on the complexity of the deliverables, the required facility clearance at the TOP SECRET/SCI level, and the minimum $50 million bonding threshold, the Government was unable to determine that two or more small business concerns could perform the work at a fair market price. Accordingly, a small business set-aside was considered but is not being applied to this procurement. This determination was made consistent with FAR 19.502-2 and does not reflect a negative assessment of the small business community's general capabilities. The Government anticipates that small businesses may participate as subcontractors under this vehicle. Any firm awarded a prime contract under this solicitation with a total anticipated value exceeding $750,000 must submit a subcontracting plan identifying small business subcontracting opportunities.

The General Services Administration (GSA) Office of Strategic Communication hereby solicits proposals for the configuration, customization, and deployment of a SharePoint Online-based intranet portal to replace the agency's aging on-premises collaboration environment. The Government intends to leverage the existing Microsoft 365 enterprise licensing agreement and does not require the development of proprietary software. Contractors shall provide SharePoint configuration services including taxonomy design, metadata schema development, site collection architecture, and integration with existing Azure Active Directory identity management. The scope explicitly excludes custom application development. Deliverables include a configured SharePoint environment, governance documentation, administrator training, and a phased migration plan for approximately 12,000 legacy content items. Offerors must demonstrate a minimum of three years of experience deploying SharePoint as a commercial solution within federal government environments and shall provide three relevant past performance references from federal civilian agencies.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of Elementary and Secondary Education (OESE), is seeking contractor support for the implementation of a grants management tracking and stakeholder communication system. The Government has completed its acquisition planning and determined that a commercial off-the-shelf solution built upon the Salesforce platform provides the optimal balance of cost, functionality, and security posture. The contractor shall configure Salesforce to support grant application intake, reviewer assignment workflows, award notification processes, and recipient reporting tracking. Integration with the Department's existing identity provider using SAML 2.0 federation is required. The contractor is explicitly prohibited from developing custom software components where configuration-based solutions exist within the platform's native capabilities. The Government will provide a licensed Salesforce environment; the contractor is responsible solely for configuration, testing, training, and go-live support. The acquisition is set aside for small businesses under NAICS 541511, though the primary deliverable remains platform configuration rather than software development.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Southwestern Division, hereby issues this solicitation for SharePoint-based knowledge management system services. The Division has standardized on Microsoft SharePoint Online as its enterprise collaboration platform under the Army's existing M365 enterprise agreement and requires a contractor to configure and deploy a structured knowledge repository supporting engineering project documentation, lessons learned capture, and cross-district information sharing. The contractor shall design and implement a SharePoint site collection hierarchy, configure content types, managed metadata term stores, search schema, and retention policies consistent with Army Records Management requirements. The Government does not require and will not fund proprietary software development under this task order. The contractor shall provide all configuration through SharePoint's native administrative interfaces and approved Power Platform tools. Training shall be provided for up to 60 knowledge managers across six district offices. Final deliverables include a fully operational configured SharePoint environment and comprehensive administrator and end-user documentation.

The United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Farm Production and Conservation Business Center is issuing this solicitation for enterprise collaboration environment migration services. The Government intends to transition approximately 8,500 users from a legacy on-premises file sharing and document management infrastructure to a modern cloud-based collaboration environment leveraging the USDA's existing Microsoft 365 enterprise license agreement. The contractor shall configure and deploy SharePoint Online as the commercial off-the-shelf platform underpinning the new collaboration environment, consistent with Microsoft's published government community cloud architecture standards. Configuration scope includes information architecture design, document library taxonomy, retention label policy implementation, external sharing governance controls, and integration with existing Microsoft Teams channels and Azure Active Directory group structures. The Government explicitly does not require and will not fund any custom application development, custom web parts requiring original code authoring, or third-party software procurement under this effort. The contractor shall deliver a phased migration plan addressing approximately 40 terabytes of legacy content, execute two pilot migrations covering selected organizational units prior to enterprise rollout, and provide SharePoint administrator training for a cohort of fifteen designated agency personnel. Offerors shall hold a Microsoft Solutions Partner designation in Modern Work and shall submit a minimum of three past performance references from federal government SharePoint Online deployments completed within the last five years. The base period of performance is twenty-four months.

PART I – BACKGROUND AND PROGRAM HISTORY

The Department has administered socioeconomic contracting programs for several decades, consistent with the Small Business Act and subsequent legislative amendments. Historically, a portion of the agency's service contracts were awarded through the SBA's 8(a) Business Development Program, which provides contracting assistance to small disadvantaged businesses meeting eligibility criteria established at 13 CFR Part 124. The incumbent contract for facilities management support services at the Northfield Campus was awarded competitively under the 8(a) program in Fiscal Year 2018. However, upon completion of the statutory nine-year program term applicable to 8(a) participants, and following the incumbent firm's graduation from the 8(a) program, the agency is no longer eligible to re-procure this requirement under 8(a) authority. This successor acquisition has therefore been structured as an unrestricted procurement open to all business categories. Offerors should note that prior performance evaluations referencing the previous 8(a) contract are included in the background documentation solely as historical context and do not indicate any preference or set-aside status applicable to the current solicitation. The Government anticipates awarding a single Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity contract as a result of this solicitation.

Example 1:
The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) will conduct oral presentations with competitive range offerors as an integral component of the source selection evaluation for this Investigative Support Services contract. Oral presentations are designed to enable the Government to evaluate the offeror's technical capabilities, proposed methodologies, and the experience of key personnel in a direct interactive format. The FBI will schedule oral presentations at Headquarters in Washington, D.C., and travel costs shall be borne by the offeror. Each presentation shall be strictly limited to sixty (60) minutes, followed by a thirty (30) minute question-and-answer period facilitated by the Source Selection Advisory Council. The offeror's proposed Program Manager must lead the presentation and respond to technical questions. All materials used during the oral presentation must be classified no higher than the level specified in the DD Form 254 attached hereto. Offerors must submit all presentation materials through the designated secure portal no later than seventy-two (72) hours prior to their scheduled session. Evaluation results from the oral presentation will be documented and retained as part of the official source selection record.

Example 2:
As part of the evaluation methodology for this Information Technology Services procurement, the General Services Administration (GSA) will incorporate oral presentations into the source selection process. After an initial review of written proposals, offerors receiving a rating of Acceptable or higher under the Technical Factor will be invited to participate in an oral presentation before the Technical Evaluation Team. The oral presentation allows the Government to assess offeror capabilities beyond what is conveyed in written form and to evaluate proposed personnel directly. Each offeror will be allotted ninety (90) minutes total: sixty (60) minutes for the presentation and thirty (30) minutes for Government-directed questions. Key personnel named in Section H must be present and actively participate. Substitutions of key personnel for the oral presentation will not be permitted without prior written approval and may negatively impact the evaluation. Offerors should note that the oral presentation is evaluated separately under Factor 2 – Management Approach and scored on an Adjectival basis consistent with the criteria described in Section M.

Example 3:
In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

RFP chunk to analyze:
3.4 EVALUATION METHODOLOGY

The Government intends to conduct a streamlined source selection in accordance with FAR 15.101-2, Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) procedures. Evaluation of proposals will be conducted exclusively through written submissions. Offerors shall not prepare or anticipate any oral presentation of their technical approach, past performance history, or management plan. All information necessary for a complete evaluation must be contained within the written proposal submitted by the due date established in Section L. The Contracting Officer has determined that oral presentations would not contribute meaningfully to the evaluation process given the well-defined, commoditized nature of the requirement. Accordingly, the Government reserves no right to request demonstrations, briefings, or in-person discussions as part of the formal evaluation. Any questions regarding proposal requirements must be submitted in writing via the designated portal no later than the question-and-answer deadline. Responses will be posted as an amendment to the solicitation and made available to all prospective offerors. The written technical volume, price volume, and past performance volume, as outlined in Section L, constitute the complete basis for award. Offerors who contact Government personnel outside the established written channels may be deemed non-responsive.

The General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Acquisition Service seeks a contractor to provide software development and maintenance support for the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

Example 3:
The Social Security Administration (SSA) announces a competitive procurement for modernization and operations support services for the Disability Case Processing System (DCPS). Contractors responding to this solicitation must understand that this effort constitutes continuing work on a system first placed into production in 2016. The existing codebase, comprising front-end Angular components and back-end services built on IBM WebSphere, will be transferred to the incoming contractor in its current state. SSA does not anticipate a full redesign during the base period; rather, the contractor is expected to maintain, enhance, and incrementally modernize the existing system in accordance with the Technology Modernization Plan attached as Exhibit C. The Government will facilitate a structured transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, providing access to all repository assets, architectural documentation, and operations runbooks. Proposals must include a staffing plan that accounts for immediate knowledge absorption from outgoing personnel during a 120-day transition period.

Following written proposal evaluation, offerors deemed within the competitive range will be invited to deliver an oral presentation to the Source Selection Evaluation Board (SSEB). The oral presentation is intended to allow offerors to clarify and elaborate upon their written technical submissions and to demonstrate the depth of understanding of the program requirements. Presentations shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. Remote participation via video teleconference will be permitted only upon written approval by the Contracting Officer. Scores assigned during the oral presentation phase will be integrated with written proposal scores to produce a final technical rating for each offeror.

To be eligible to receive award under this solicitation, offerors must (1) be certified as a HUBZone small business concern by the SBA, (2) be registered in SAM with an active HUBZone designation, and (3) continue to qualify as a HUBZone small business through the duration of contract performance. The NAICS code for this acquisition is 541490, with a size standard of $19.0 million. Offerors must include their SBA-issued HUBZone certification number in their proposal cover letter. The Government will not consider offers from firms that do not hold an active HUBZone certification. Estimated contract value is $2.4 million over a base year plus four option years.

The Social Security Administration requires all IT development work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement to comply with SSA's Agile Development Standards. Offerors shall describe their Agile delivery model in the technical approach and demonstrate how their proposed methodology aligns with Scrum or Kanban frameworks. Contractors shall conduct sprint planning meetings with SSA's Product Owner to decompose epics into sprint-ready user stories with acceptance criteria no later than the iteration planning session preceding each sprint. The contractor shall update the Government's enterprise project management tool daily with sprint board status, impediments, and burndown data. Example 2:
In accordance with the Department of Homeland Security's Digital Services Strategy, all software engineering work performed under this contract shall follow Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) practices. The contractor shall conduct Program Increment (PI) Planning events every ten weeks, bringing together all Agile Release Train teams to align on objectives and identify dependencies. Each Agile team shall operate in two-week iterations with clearly defined iteration goals established during iteration planning ceremonies. The contractor shall designate Release Train Engineers to facilitate ART-level events and support teams in removing cross-team impediments. The System Demo, conducted at the end of each PI, shall be open to all Government stakeholders and demonstrate integrated working software. The contractor shall maintain and update Team and Program backlogs within the Government's approved Agile lifecycle management tooling. Inspect and Adapt workshops shall be conducted at the close of each PI to drive continuous improvement and quantify the improvement backlog.

Example 3:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services requires the contractor to apply Agile methodology across all workstreams supporting the modernization of legacy claims processing systems. The contractor shall organize work into user stories, epics, and themes within a hierarchical backlog structure and shall demonstrate traceability from individual user stories to program-level epics and strategic objectives. Sprints shall be conducted on a two-week cadence with sprint planning occurring on the first day of each sprint and sprint review and retrospective conducted on the final day. The Government's designated Product Owner shall have authority to accept or reject sprint deliverables based on the agreed Definition of Done. The contractor shall not carry incomplete user stories across sprint boundaries without documented justification and re-estimation. The contractor shall facilitate quarterly Agile maturity assessments using a Government-approved framework and shall present findings and improvement recommendations to the CMS program office. Test-Driven Development (TDD) and Continuous Integration practices shall be employed as integral components of the Agile engineering workflow.

The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor shall implement all five Scrum events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint itself, with Government personnel invited and encouraged to participate in Sprint Reviews and Retrospectives. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. The contractor shall establish and maintain Agile teams with a recommended team size of five to nine members, each including a dedicated Scrum Master and supported by a Government-designated Product Owner. User stories shall be written with clearly defined acceptance criteria using the standard 'As a [user], I want [functionality], so that [benefit]' format and shall be estimated using Planning Poker or equivalent relative sizing techniques. The contractor shall operate on a consistent two-week sprint cadence and shall not alter sprint scope once the sprint has commenced without documented consent from the Contracting Officer's Representative. Sprint retrospective outputs shall be shared with the Government and incorporated into the team's continuous improvement plan. Quarterly roadmaps reflecting planned feature delivery shall be maintained and updated following each sprint review.

Sprint velocity data, cumulative flow diagrams, and release burnup charts shall be included in the contractor's monthly progress report. Example 1:
The Small Business Administration, Office of Disaster Assistance, requires data entry and document processing support services for its Fort Worth, Texas processing center. This acquisition is structured as a Time-and-Materials task order to be placed against an existing SBA Indefinite Delivery contract. The total funded amount for this task order is $93,000, representing the maximum labor and other direct cost reimbursement available. The Government has budgeted no contingency above this figure. Offerors must propose labor categories and hourly rates that, when multiplied by the estimated 2,400 hours of performance, do not exceed the $93,000 ceiling inclusive of all applicable materials and ODCs. The period of performance is six months. Proposals must include a detailed staffing plan and a cost narrative demonstrating how all tasks will be completed within the ninety-three thousand dollar ($93,000) total funded value. Submissions exceeding this ceiling will be rejected without further evaluation.

Example 2:
The National Park Service, Intermountain Region, issues this Request for Proposals for interpretive signage design and fabrication services at a designated historic district within Capitol Reef National Park. The Government anticipates awarding a single firm-fixed-price purchase order with a total value not to exceed $62,000. This figure encompasses all design labor, graphic production, materials, fabrication, site delivery, and installation activities. The NPS has conducted market research confirming that the described scope of work is achievable within this funding level. Offerors that cannot perform all required tasks within the $62,000 ceiling are encouraged not to submit proposals, as the agency has no flexibility to increase this award amount. The period of performance is one hundred and twenty (120) days after issuance of the purchase order. This acquisition is 100% set aside for small businesses. Quoters must provide a detailed cost breakdown demonstrating that total proposed costs, inclusive of all direct and indirect expenses and profit or fee, do not surpass sixty-two thousand dollars ($62,000).

Example 3:
The Department of Commerce, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, Ocean Service, seeks a qualified vendor to provide professional editing, layout, and print-ready production services for NOAA's annual State of the Coast report, encompassing approximately 120 pages of technical narrative, infographics, and photography. This acquisition will be executed as a firm-fixed-price purchase order. The maximum obligated amount for these services is $41,200, which covers all editorial labor, graphic design, accessibility compliance review, and file preparation across the six-month period of performance. NOAA has no additional discretionary funds available for this publication and has structured the scope of work specifically to be executable within the forty-one thousand two hundred dollar ($41,200) ceiling. Vendors must propose pricing for all services described in the statement of work, and total proposed pricing must fall at or below $41,200. Offerors are discouraged from proposing optional enhancements or add-on services that would increase cost above the available budget. Award will be made to the lowest-priced technically acceptable offeror whose total price is within the stated funding constraint.

This amount encompasses all labor, cleaning supplies, equipment, supervision, and associated overhead and profit. Offerors must submit a pricing schedule that clearly itemizes recurring monthly costs and any one-time startup costs, with the cumulative total not surpassing the contract maximum. Offers proposing a total price in excess of $91,000 will be deemed ineligible for award.

The General Services Administration, Federal Acquisition Service, Region 6, issues this combined synopsis and solicitation for janitorial and custodial services at a single federal office building in Kansas City, Missouri, containing approximately 8,400 rentable square feet. The contract will be structured as a firm-fixed-price arrangement for a base year with no options. The maximum contract value, inclusive of all labor, supplies, equipment, and supervision for the twelve-month base period, is $38,500. This figure reflects the agency's available appropriations for facility maintenance services at this location. Prospective vendors must be capable of providing five-day-per-week service within this funding constraint. The GSA contracting officer has documented that market research supports the feasibility of fulfilling the described services within the $38,500 ceiling based on comparable awards at similar-sized facilities. Quoters whose proposed annual cost exceeds thirty-eight thousand five hundred dollars ($38,500) will be eliminated from consideration. Award will be made on a lowest-price, technically acceptable basis.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs, Office of Information and Technology, hereby solicits proposals for the development and deployment of a patient scheduling optimization tool for use at one regional VA medical center. The total ceiling for this contract shall not exceed $85,000 for a base period of performance of twelve (12) months. No option periods are anticipated at this time. Offerors are advised that all technical deliverables, including requirements documentation, system design, development, testing, training, and post-deployment support, must be accomplished within the stated ceiling amount. The Government does not intend to award a contract exceeding this threshold under any circumstances. This acquisition is set aside exclusively for small businesses under NAICS code 541511. Interested parties should note that the contracting officer has determined that $85,000 represents a fair and reasonable price for the described scope of work based on independent government cost estimates. Proposals that exceed this maximum budget will be deemed non-responsive and will not be evaluated further. Offerors must clearly demonstrate how all required tasks can be completed within the $85,000 total contract value.

Example 3:
NOTICE OF INTENT TO AWARD / REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS – DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE, BUREAU OF PRISONS. The Federal Bureau of Prisons, Facilities Management Branch, solicits proposals for the repair and restoration of exterior masonry at one medium-security correctional facility in the Southeast Region. The scope includes inspection, joint repointing, surface cleaning, and application of waterproof sealant across approximately 4,000 square feet of affected surface area. This procurement will result in a single award firm-fixed-price contract. Available funding for this effort totals $88,000, which constitutes the full and complete contract ceiling. The BOP has determined that this project must be executed within that amount based on current capital improvement budget allocations for the facility. No modifications adding additional scope or funding will be entertained after award. Contractors must include a complete material and labor cost breakdown with their proposals and certify that the total proposed price, inclusive of all overhead, profit, bonding, and insurance, does not exceed eighty-eight thousand dollars ($88,000). Proposals above this ceiling will be rejected.

Example 1:
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) anticipates conducting oral presentations as a component of the proposal evaluation process for this acquisition. Offerors should be prepared to present their Mission Suitability approach, including their understanding of technical requirements, proposed solutions, and risk mitigation strategies. The oral presentation will be evaluated under the Mission Suitability factor and will contribute to the overall technical score. NASA will notify offerors in the competitive range of their scheduled presentation date and time no fewer than five (5) business days in advance. Each presentation session shall not exceed two (2) hours. The presenting team must include the proposed Principal Investigator and Project Manager. Offerors are advised that statements made during the oral presentation are binding and will be incorporated into the resulting contract. Any commitments made verbally that differ from the written proposal will be flagged for clarification prior to award. Visual aids and demonstrations may be used but must be pre-approved by the Contracting Officer.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) intends to conduct oral presentations with offerors as part of its evaluation of proposals submitted in response to this solicitation for Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Support Services. Oral presentations will be conducted following the initial screening of written proposals. Only those offerors determined to be technically acceptable after written review will be scheduled for an oral presentation. The oral presentation will focus primarily on the offeror's technical methodology, data management approach, and the qualifications of proposed scientific personnel. Each session is limited to one hundred and twenty (120) minutes, including a thirty (30) minute question period. The proposed Principal Investigator must personally attend and lead the oral presentation. Additional team members may participate at the offeror's discretion but are subject to a five-person maximum. Materials presented must not introduce new technical approaches not previously described in the written proposal, and any inconsistencies identified will be evaluated unfavorably.

Example 3:
In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Proposal Preparation Instructions – Oral Presentations. The National Science Foundation (NSF), Office of Polar Programs, will require competitive range offerors to deliver oral presentations as a distinct phase of the source selection evaluation for this logistics support services acquisition. NSF will schedule oral presentations following the written proposal evaluation phase and will provide each offeror with a minimum of seven (7) calendar days advance notice of their assigned presentation slot. Oral presentations will be conducted at NSF headquarters in Alexandria, Virginia, and shall not exceed eighty (80) minutes per offeror, with the final twenty (20) minutes reserved exclusively for NSF evaluator questions. The offeror's proposed Principal Investigator and Field Operations Manager must lead the presentation and must be the same individuals identified in the written technical proposal. Substitution of these personnel, either for the oral presentation or upon contract award, will be treated as a significant weakness under the Key Personnel evaluation factor. Offerors shall structure their oral presentations to address, at minimum, the following topics: technical approach to remote logistics operations, safety management framework, environmental compliance strategy, and past performance relevance. Presentation slides and any supplemental handouts must be delivered electronically to the Contracting Officer no later than forty-eight (48) hours prior to the session. Verbal statements and commitments made during the oral presentation are considered part of the offeror's proposal of record and will be binding upon award. NSF evaluators will assign independent scores to the oral presentation, which will be combined with written evaluation scores to yield a final composite Technical rating for each offeror in accordance with Section M.2.

Veterans Health Administration
Solicitation No. VA-258-24-R-0441

TYPE OF SET-ASIDE: HUBZone Set-Aside

Pursuant to the authority granted under FAR Subpart 19.13, this requirement has been designated as a HUBZone small business set-aside. The Government anticipates awarding a firm-fixed-price contract for medical transcription services to a concern that qualifies as a HUBZone small business. In order to be eligible for award, the offeror must be listed in the System for Award Management (SAM) as a certified HUBZone small business and must hold a valid HUBZone certification issued by the U.S. Small Business Administration at the time of submission. Solicitation No. AG-84S8-S-24-0018
Project: Timber Stand Improvement and Reforestation Services

SET-ASIDE NOTICE: This acquisition is set aside 100% for HUBZone small business concerns in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.13. The Forest Service has determined, based on thorough market research, that this requirement meets the criteria for a HUBZone small business set-aside. Prospective offerors must be certified HUBZone small business concerns at the time proposals are submitted and must remain certified through the date of award. Certification must be current and reflected in both the SBA HUBZone database and SAM.gov at proposal closing. NAICS code 113310 applies with a size standard of $47.0 million in annual receipts. Natural Resources Conservation Service
Request for Proposals
Solicitation No. USDA-NRCS-2024-Q-0053

SECTION B — SUPPLIES OR SERVICES AND PRICES
B.1 SET-ASIDE INFORMATION

In accordance with Federal Acquisition Regulation 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole Source Award, this solicitation is set aside exclusively for Historically Underutilized Business Zone (HUBZone) small business concerns. The Natural Resources Conservation Service seeks to procure geospatial mapping and soil survey data collection services to support conservation planning activities across designated rural counties in the southeastern United States.

Eligibility for award under this solicitation is restricted to offerors that hold a valid SBA HUBZone certification as of the proposal due date. Certification must remain active and in good standing at the time of contract award. Offerors are responsible for ensuring their HUBZone certification has not lapsed or been suspended; the Government assumes no obligation to notify offerors of certification deficiencies discovered during the evaluation process.

The NAICS code applicable to this acquisition is 541370 (Surveying and Mapping Services), size standard $19.0 million. In setting aside this requirement, the contracting officer has made a reasonable expectation determination that two or more responsible HUBZone small business concerns will submit offers at prices that are fair and reasonable to the Government. Award will be made on a best-value basis considering technical capability, past performance, and price. The HUBZone prime contractor must perform no less than 50 percent of the cost of contract performance incurred for personnel, as required by 13 C.F.R. § 126.700. Questions regarding HUBZone certification eligibility should be directed to the SBA HUBZone Program at (202) 205-8885 prior to the proposal submission deadline.

AGRICULTURAL RESEARCH SERVICE

LABORATORY SUPPLIES AND CONSUMABLES

SET-ASIDE TYPE: Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB)

The United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service, Western Regional Research Center, hereby announces this solicitation as a Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) set-aside pursuant to FAR Subpart 19.15. Only firms that are currently certified as Women-Owned Small Business concerns through the U.S. Small Business Administration shall be considered for award under this solicitation. The requirement encompasses the acquisition of laboratory consumables, reagents, personal protective equipment, and general scientific supplies on an indefinite delivery, indefinite quantity basis. NAICS Code 423490 (Other Professional and Commercial Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers) applies, with a corresponding size standard of 100 employees. The estimated minimum and maximum order quantities are $50,000 and $500,000, respectively, over the base period of performance. Option years may extend the contract up to five total years. Offerors shall provide evidence of active WOSB certification in the SAM.gov and certify.sba.gov systems as part of their quote package. Quotes that omit certification evidence or that are submitted by firms whose WOSB certification has lapsed will not be considered. See the attached Statement of Work and Attachment 1 for the full catalog of required items and specifications.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE
DEFENSE LOGISTICS AGENCY
SOLICITATION: SPE600-24-R-0412
FOOD SERVICE MANAGEMENT

This solicitation is issued as a Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) set-aside. In accordance with FAR 19.1505 and the authority granted under the Small Business Act, the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support has restricted this acquisition for food service management and dining facility operations to WOSB concerns only. Market research confirmed that a reasonable expectation exists that at least two responsible WOSB firms will submit offers and that award will be made at a fair and reasonable price within the meaning of FAR 19.1505(b). The NAICS code for this procurement is 722310 (Food Service Contractors) and the applicable size standard is $47.0 million in average annual receipts. Firms must be certified as Women-Owned Small Businesses through the SBA's certify.sba.gov portal and maintain that certification throughout the contract period. The contract will cover food service operations at two military installations in the Mid-Atlantic region, with a base period of performance of one year and four one-year option periods. The Government reserves the right to cancel this set-aside if it determines that insufficient competition exists among WOSB concerns. See Section L for complete proposal preparation instructions.

SOLICITATION NO. DTFH6124R00034
TRAFFIC DATA COLLECTION AND ANALYSIS

Technical evaluation factors are described in full in Section M of this solicitation package.

The period of performance shall be six (6) months from the date of contract execution, with no provisions for renewal or extension under this solicitation. Given the short-term nature of this requirement, offerors are advised that no option years will be exercised. Award will be made to the offeror representing the best value to the Government using a tradeoff between technical merit and price.

SOLICITATION NOTICE: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), Goddard Space Flight Center, Office of Procurement, is seeking a qualified contractor to support the development and delivery of prototype sensor calibration hardware for the Earth Science Division. The period of performance for this effort is limited to eight (8) months from date of award. This is a firm-fixed-price, short-term developmental contract and does not include option periods. The contractor shall deliver a minimum of three functional prototype units, all associated technical documentation, test reports, and a lessons-learned summary. All deliverables must be submitted to the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative no later than the final month of performance. Milestone reviews are scheduled at months two, five, and seven. Proposals shall be evaluated on technical capability, schedule feasibility, and price.

Example 1:
This Performance Work Statement requires the contractor to execute all software design, development, and testing activities using Agile Scrum methodology in strict compliance with the agency's DevSecOps and Agile Transformation Initiative. The contractor shall establish and maintain Agile teams with a recommended team size of five to nine members, each including a dedicated Scrum Master and supported by a Government-designated Product Owner. User stories shall be written with clearly defined acceptance criteria using the standard 'As a [user], I want [functionality], so that [benefit]' format and shall be estimated using Planning Poker or equivalent relative sizing techniques. The contractor shall operate on a consistent two-week sprint cadence and shall not alter sprint scope once the sprint has commenced without documented consent from the Contracting Officer's Representative. Sprint retrospective outputs shall be shared with the Government and incorporated into the team's continuous improvement plan. Quarterly roadmaps reflecting planned feature delivery shall be maintained and updated following each sprint review.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, requires the contractor to lead and execute a modernization initiative using Agile Scrum practices in alignment with the agency's Technology Modernization Playbook. The contractor shall staff an Agile delivery team that includes at minimum a Scrum Master, Product Manager, UX Researcher, Front-End and Back-End Developers, and a Quality Assurance Engineer. All work shall be organized into a continuously refined product backlog with epics, features, and user stories sized and prioritized by the Government Product Owner in collaboration with the contractor. Sprint cycles shall be two weeks in duration, and the contractor shall present a sprint demo to agency stakeholders at each sprint review, with attendance tracked and documented. The Scrum Master shall facilitate sprint retrospectives and shall maintain a retrospective action log accessible to the Government. Agile metrics shall be reported in the contractor's monthly status report, including planned versus completed story points, sprint goal achievement rate, and the number of impediments raised and resolved. The contractor shall provide Agile coaching support to Government personnel as a secondary deliverable to build internal agency capacity.

Example 3:
The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

SECTION L.12 – TECHNICAL APPROACH REQUIREMENTS. Offerors responding to this solicitation issued by the Department of Transportation, Federal Transit Administration, shall describe their approach to Agile software development methodology in the Technical Volume of their proposal. FTA requires that the contractor utilize Scrum as the primary delivery framework for all development efforts associated with the Transit Safety Data Analytics Platform. Proposals that do not demonstrate a clear and credible Agile/Scrum delivery model will be considered technically unacceptable. The contractor's technical approach shall address team composition and Scrum roles, including how Scrum Master responsibilities will be fulfilled and how the team will interface with the FTA-designated Product Owner for backlog management and sprint acceptance. Upon award, the contractor shall deliver an Agile Project Management Plan within fifteen calendar days, documenting sprint cadence, ceremony schedule, Definition of Ready, Definition of Done, and the tooling to be used for backlog and sprint tracking. The Government will maintain Product Owner authority over prioritization decisions, and the contractor shall not commence development on any user story absent Product Owner acceptance into the sprint backlog. Sprint reviews shall be scheduled with a minimum of five business days' notice to FTA stakeholders and shall include a live demonstration of all completed user stories against their respective acceptance criteria. The contractor shall implement continuous integration practices consistent with Agile engineering principles, ensuring that working software is available in the FTA development environment at the close of each sprint. Agile coaching services shall be available to the team upon Government request to support adherence to Scrum values and continuous improvement practices across the delivery lifecycle.

SOLICITATION NUMBER: HHS-2024-EDWOSB-0042

This acquisition is a 100% set-aside for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns under the authority of 15 U.S.C. 637(m). The Department of Health and Human Services, Office of Acquisition Services, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for the provision of professional health information technology consulting services in support of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services modernization initiative. Only offerors who are certified Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns at the time of offer submission and at the time of award are eligible to receive this contract. Offerors must be registered in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) and must have a current EDWOSB certification through the SBA certification program or a third-party certifier approved by the Small Business Administration. The applicable NAICS code is 541512 with a small business size standard of $34 million in average annual receipts. Any offer received from a concern that does not qualify as an EDWOSB at the time of offer will be considered nonresponsive and will not be evaluated. Questions regarding EDWOSB eligibility requirements should be directed to the contracting officer prior to the closing date of this solicitation.

Example 2:
U.S. ARMY CORPS OF ENGINEERS
SOLICITATION NO.: W912DY-24-R-0055
SET-ASIDE TYPE: EDWOSB

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Louisville District, is soliciting offers under a total Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside for architectural and engineering services related to the design and rehabilitation of flood control infrastructure in the Ohio River watershed. This procurement is conducted in accordance with the Brooks Act (40 U.S.C. 1101 et seq.) and the Small Business Act's EDWOSB provisions at 15 U.S.C. 637(m). The NAICS code applicable to this requirement is 541330 – Engineering Services, with a size standard of $25.5 million. Only EDWOSB-certified firms may submit qualifications packages in response to this announcement. To qualify, the concern must be at least 51 percent unconditionally and directly owned and controlled by one or more women who are economically disadvantaged as defined by 13 CFR 127.200. Firms must demonstrate active EDWOSB certification through the SBA's certification platform (certify.sba.gov) or an approved third-party certifier. Failure to maintain EDWOSB certification through the date of award shall result in the offeror being deemed ineligible. Subcontracting to non-EDWOSB firms is permitted in accordance with applicable FAR provisions, provided the EDWOSB prime contractor performs the required percentage of work.

OFFICE OF MISSION SUPPORT
Solicitation No.: EPA-MSD-24-00156-EDWOSB

PURSUANT TO THE WOMEN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESS FEDERAL CONTRACTING PROGRAM, this solicitation is designated as a 100 percent set-aside for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) intends to procure data analytics and environmental monitoring software development services in support of the Office of Water's National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) program. NAICS code 541511 – Custom Computer Programming Services applies to this requirement, with a small business size standard of $34 million in annual revenue. The EPA's contracting officer has made an affirmative determination that setting aside this requirement exclusively for EDWOSB concerns is appropriate based on thorough market research conducted in accordance with FAR 10.001. To participate, an offeror must be certified as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business by the SBA or an approved third-party certifier and must be registered and active in SAM.gov. Offerors must upload their EDWOSB certification documentation to the secure file-sharing portal identified in Section L of this solicitation no later than the proposal due date. Award will not be made to any offeror that cannot demonstrate a valid, current EDWOSB certification at the time of award.

The contract will be structured as a Firm Fixed Price vehicle with a base development period of twenty-four months and a twelve-month post-deployment support option. The contractor shall propose a staffing structure appropriate to the scope of work, including identified leads for software development, quality assurance, data integration, and security compliance. Deliverables include a concept of operations document, detailed design specification, data migration strategy, test plans and results, privacy impact assessment, system security plan, and an authority to operate package. The contractor shall conduct joint application design sessions with FSA business owners and technical staff at intervals determined during project kickoff. Source code and all related development artifacts shall become property of the Government upon delivery. Proposals will be evaluated on technical merit, past performance, and price under a best value tradeoff methodology.

This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 2:
This solicitation is issued by the General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, for grounds maintenance and landscaping services at federal facilities in the National Capital Region. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) methodology as prescribed in FAR 15.101-2. Evaluation factors include Technical Capability and Price. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

Example 3:

The scope of work encompasses system architecture redesign, application development, data migration, testing and quality assurance, training, and help desk support. Based on the complexity and mission-critical nature of this effort, the VA requires the contractor to provide a dedicated project team of no less than twenty (20) full-time personnel. The staffing complement shall include, at a minimum, a Program Manager, an Agile Release Train Engineer, five (5) software developers with demonstrated Java and cloud platform experience, three (3) database administrators, two (2) DevSecOps engineers, a cybersecurity SME, two (2) business analysts, a training lead, and requisite quality assurance personnel. The required staffing configuration shall include a Program Manager with PMP certification, a Deputy Program Manager, a Help Desk Manager overseeing a staff of at least four (4) Tier I and Tier II support specialists, three (3) application developers, two (2) database administrators, a network engineer, a cybersecurity analyst, a data warehouse specialist, and a configuration management specialist. The contractor shall maintain this minimum staffing level throughout all base and option periods. The staffing plan, including résumés for all eighteen key and non-key personnel, shall be submitted with Volume II of the technical proposal.

DISTRICT OPERATIONS SUPPORT

The U.S. Small Business Administration, Office of Administrative Services, is seeking administrative and clerical support staffing services for SBA district offices in the Mid-Atlantic region, including offices located in Maryland, Virginia, Delaware, and the District of Columbia. The applicable NAICS code is 561110 (Office Administrative Services) with a size standard of $12 million. Only offerors that certify their status as a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business in SAM.gov and comply with the ownership and control requirements of 13 CFR Part 128 are eligible to receive an award under this solicitation. In evaluating offers, the contracting officer will verify SDVOSB certifications prior to any award decision. Concerns that cannot demonstrate compliance with the definition of a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business as stated in FAR 2.101 will be found ineligible for award. The government anticipates awarding a single-award time-and-materials task order contract with a one-year base period and up to three one-year option periods.

DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE
FOREST SERVICE
SOLICITATION NUMBER: 12-FS-24-R-0094

The United States Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Pacific Northwest Region, hereby issues this solicitation as a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) set-aside in accordance with FAR 19.1405 and the clause at FAR 52.219-27. This procurement covers reforestation, timber stand improvement, and invasive species removal services within designated National Forest lands in Oregon and Washington. The North American Industry Classification System code is 115310 (Support Activities for Forestry) with a size standard of $9 million. Consistent with the FAR requirements applicable to SDVOSB set-asides, offers will only be accepted from businesses that have certified their status as a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). The contracting officer has determined there is a reasonable expectation of receiving offers from two or more responsible SDVOSB concerns capable of performing the work at competitive prices. Contract type is firm-fixed-price. Performance bonds and liability insurance in amounts specified in the solicitation are required from the successful offeror prior to contract execution.

NATIONAL NUCLEAR SECURITY ADMINISTRATION

THIS ACQUISITION IS A TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESS (SDVOSB) FIRMS. Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, solicits proposals for the modernization of the Personnel Security Adjudication Platform. The Government mandates use of a traditional SDLC approach consistent with DHS policy directives governing enterprise system development. The contractor shall execute the project through clearly delineated sequential phases: Concept of Operations, Requirements Definition, Preliminary Design, Critical Design, Code and Unit Test, Integration and Test, System Acceptance Test, and Operational Deployment. A formal phase gate review shall be conducted at the conclusion of each phase, with Government approval required before work proceeds. Requirements established at the conclusion of the Requirements Definition phase shall constitute the fixed requirements baseline and shall be placed under configuration management control. No development work shall begin prior to Government approval of the System Requirements Specification document.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

Formal phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and conducted at the conclusion of each phase. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan, integrated master schedule, and deliverable acceptance plan within 30 calendar days of contract award.

This solicitation for Healthcare Information Technology Modernization Support Services includes an oral presentation requirement as a formal element of the evaluation process. The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) will invite offerors in the competitive range to participate in structured oral presentations following the initial written proposal review. Oral presentations will be assessed under Technical Factor 2 – Implementation Methodology and Technical Factor 4 – Relevant Experience. Each offeror will have seventy-five (75) minutes to present, followed by a twenty-five (25) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the HHS Evaluation Committee. The offeror's proposed Program Manager, Lead Systems Architect, and at least one clinical informatics specialist must be present in person. Remote participation is not authorized except under emergency circumstances approved in advance by the Contracting Officer. Offerors should structure their oral presentation to directly address the evaluation criteria outlined in Section M and are advised that new information introduced during the oral presentation that was not contained in the written proposal will be evaluated as a clarification and not as a revision or amendment to the offer.

Example 2:
Section M – Evaluation Criteria: The Department of Homeland Security will evaluate all responsive proposals using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology. Following written proposal evaluation, offerors deemed within the competitive range will be invited to deliver an oral presentation to the Source Selection Evaluation Board (SSEB). The oral presentation is intended to allow offerors to clarify and elaborate upon their written technical submissions and to demonstrate the depth of understanding of the program requirements. Presentations shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. The lead proposed Program Manager and at least two key technical personnel identified in the proposal must participate in person. Remote participation via video teleconference will be permitted only upon written approval by the Contracting Officer. Scores assigned during the oral presentation phase will be integrated with written proposal scores to produce a final technical rating for each offeror.

Example 3:

The oral presentation will be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Network Contracting Office and will be scheduled at the Government's discretion. Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. All slide materials or visual aids must be provided to the Contracting Officer no later than three (3) business days prior to the presentation. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

Example 1:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

Example 2:
The Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, issues this solicitation for tree marking, timber cruising, and silvicultural services in the Tongass National Forest, Alaska. Source selection will be accomplished using the lowest price technically acceptable methodology. The Technical Factor will assess whether the offeror has demonstrated the minimum qualifications, past relevant experience, and equipment necessary to perform the work as described in the Statement of Work. Technical acceptability determinations will be made on a pass/fail basis only. Price will be evaluated as the total price for all contract line items over the ordering period. Among all offerors determined to be technically acceptable, the award will be issued to the offeror presenting the lowest price. The Government has determined that LPTA is appropriate for this acquisition because the work is routine in nature, performance requirements are clearly defined, and successful performance risk is low. Offerors are cautioned that elaborate technical proposals will not improve their competitive standing.

Example 3:
The Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection, is soliciting offers for the procurement of personal protective equipment including ballistic helmets, body armor, and tactical gloves for field personnel stationed along the Southwest Border. This acquisition will utilize the lowest price technically acceptable source selection approach. Technical acceptability shall be established through compliance with all mandatory specifications contained in Section C of this solicitation. Compliance with applicable NIJ and ANSI standards is required and will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis. Delivery schedule and applicable certifications must also meet minimum thresholds to be deemed technically acceptable. Among all offers receiving a technically acceptable rating, award will be made to the offeror proposing the lowest price. The Government will not conduct tradeoff analysis. The use of Kanban boards or equivalent visual management tools is required to provide real-time transparency into work-in-progress, blocked items, and completed work. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

Section C.4 – Development Methodology Requirements. The Department of Transportation, Federal Transit Administration, requires the prime contractor and all subcontractors performing software engineering activities under this contract to adhere to the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) as the governing Agile methodology for program execution. The contractor shall establish and maintain an Agile Release Train comprised of cross-functional teams responsible for delivering the Transit Data Integration System. Program Increment Planning events shall be conducted every twelve weeks and shall be open to all FTA stakeholders, including the Contracting Officer's Representative and designated program managers. During PI Planning, each Agile team shall produce team PI objectives, identify inter-team dependencies, and commit to a set of iteration goals for the upcoming program increment. Each Agile team shall operate in two-week iterations, conducting iteration planning, daily stand-ups, iteration reviews, and iteration retrospectives as standard ceremonies. The contractor shall designate a qualified Release Train Engineer responsible for facilitating ART-level events, managing the program board, and coordinating dependency resolution across teams. System Demos shall be conducted at the close of each program increment to present integrated working software to FTA stakeholders. The contractor shall maintain Team and Program Backlogs within the Government-approved lifecycle management tooling and shall ensure backlog items are groomed and estimated prior to each iteration planning event. Inspect and Adapt workshops shall be held at the conclusion of each PI, and the resulting improvement backlog shall be tracked and reported to the COR as part of the quarterly program review package.
